target.title: feat(voice-call): implement asynchronous email delivery for consult results
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During realtime voice calls, complex questions (e.g., data queries requiring tool execution) block the caller waiting for an answer that may take minutes, with no fallback if the consult times out or the caller prefers email delivery.
- **Why it matters:** Callers hang up or get silence during long-running agent consults. There's no mechanism to acknowledge the request and deliver results asynchronously.
- **What changed:** Added background email delivery for voice-call consults with two paths: (1) **email-first** — caller explicitly requests email delivery, agent acks immediately and researches in background; (2) **timeout** — if consult exceeds `backgroundConsultTimeoutMs`, ack the caller and email the result when ready. New config fields: `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled` (explicit toggle, default `false`), `backgroundConsultTimeoutMs`, `backgroundEmailPrompt`, `recipientEmail`. New file `background-email-delivery.ts` handles fire-and-forget email delivery via embedded agent runs. Added `deliveryPreference` arg to the consult tool schema. Improved system prompts to prevent narration in background runs and prevent re-querying in email delivery. Email delivery agent spawns are restricted to `["exec", "memory_search", "memory_get"]` tools only. All logs redacted to structural IDs (no args/transcript/result text). Timeout timer properly cleaned up after `Promise.race`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Normal voice consult path is untouched. No changes to the realtime voice provider, webhook server, call manager, or telephony providers. Core `consultRealtimeVoiceAgent` only gained an optional `abortSignal` param — no behavioral change. Plugin SDK barrel export additions only.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related # (none — feature request from internal testing)
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Voice call consults that exceed timeout now ack the caller and deliver results by email. Callers can also explicitly request email delivery. Feature is off by default and requires explicit opt-in via `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true`.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw dev with Twilio realtime voice, Claude (Anthropic) provider
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Configured `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true`, `backgroundConsultTimeoutMs: 15000` and `recipientEmail`, called the voice number, asked a complex data query, observed timeout path trigger and email delivery spawn
- **Evidence after fix:** Logs show `Timeout path: 15000ms exceeded`, `Email-first path: returning ack`, `Spawning email delivery agent`, `Background email delivery completed`
- **Observed result after fix:** Caller receives spoken ack ("I'll research that and send you an email"), background consult completes, email delivery agent spawns and sends email
- **What was not tested:** Tests not yet updated after iterative changes; `pnpm check:changed` not yet run
- **Before evidence:** Before this change, long consults blocked the caller indefinitely or timed out with an error

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — new feature

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `extensions/voice-call/src/runtime.background-email.test.ts`, `extensions/voice-call/src/background-email-delivery.test.ts`, `src/realtime-voice/agent-consult-tool.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** email-first path returns ack and spawns delivery; timeout path races and falls back to email; `parseRealtimeVoiceAgentConsultArgs` normalizes `deliveryPreference`; `buildRealtimeVoiceAgentConsultEmailAckResponse` returns correct ack text; both paths gated by `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled`; disabled toggle falls through to normal consult
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** Unit tests on the tool schema/args + integration test on the runtime handler paths cover all branching logic
- **If no new test is added, why not:** Test files exist but need updating after iterative changes

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config fields under `plugins.entries.voice-call.config.realtime`:
  - `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled` (boolean, default `false`) — master toggle for the entire background email delivery feature; must be `true` to enable email-first and timeout paths
  - `backgroundConsultTimeoutMs` (number, optional) — timeout before falling back to email (requires `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled`)
  - `backgroundEmailPrompt` (string, optional) — custom system prompt for email delivery agent
  - `recipientEmail` (string, email, optional) — pre-configured recipient; voice agent won't ask for email
- New `deliveryPreference` argument in the realtime voice consult tool (`"voice"` default, `"email"` for explicit email request) — only honored when `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled` is `true`
- Voice agent instructions updated to mention email delivery option
- Graceful shutdown now flushes pending background deliveries

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[caller asks question] -> [consult agent] -> [wait...] -> [timeout/error OR answer]

After (email-first, backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true):
[caller asks "email me the answer"] -> [ack immediately] -> [background consult] -> [email delivery agent] -> [email sent]

After (timeout, backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true):
[caller asks question] -> [race: consult vs timeout]
  -> consult wins: [return answer to caller]
  -> timeout wins: [ack caller] -> [consult continues] -> [email delivery agent] -> [email sent]

After (backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: false or unset):
[caller asks question] -> [consult agent] -> [return answer to caller]  (same as before)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` — email delivery uses existing exec tool (himalaya/gog CLI)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Restricted` — email delivery agent spawns are limited to `["exec", "memory_search", "memory_get"]` tools via `toolsAllow`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- PII in logs? `No` — all tool handler and email delivery logs redacted to structural IDs only (no args, transcript, or result text)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: Anthropic Claude (sonnet)
- Integration/channel: Twilio voice-call realtime
- Relevant config (redacted): `realtime.enabled: true`, `realtime.backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true`, `realtime.backgroundConsultTimeoutMs: 15000`, `realtime.recipientEmail: <redacted>`

### Steps

1. Configure voice-call plugin with `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: true`, `backgroundConsultTimeoutMs`, and `recipientEmail`
2. Call the configured phone number
3. Ask a complex question that requires tool execution (e.g., "What are January purchases by location?")
4. Observe: caller gets ack after timeout, email arrives with full answer

### Expected

- Caller receives spoken acknowledgment after timeout
- Background consult completes with full data
- Email delivery agent sends the answer

### Actual

- Confirmed via live testing with Twilio + Anthropic

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets (runtime logs showing all three paths)
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)
```text
14:34:56 [voice-call] realtime tool call executing callId=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd tool=openclaw_agent_consult hasHandler=true
14:34:56 [plugins] [voice-call] Tool handler invoked: tool=openclaw_agent_consult, call=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd, args={"question":"***","context":"User asked: \"Can you please give me a ***\"","responseStyle":"warm, friendly, professional","deliveryPreference":"voice"}, partialTranscript=null
14:34:56 [plugins] [voice-call] Timeout path: racing consult against 15000ms for call=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd, agent=main, session=voice:573042634248
14:35:11 [plugins] [voice-call] Timeout path: 15000ms exceeded for call=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd, deferring to email delivery
14:35:11 [voice-call] realtime tool call completed callId=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd tool=openclaw_agent_consult status=ok elapsedMs=15009
14:35:43 [plugins] [voice-call] Timeout path: background consult resolved for call=4b560e0d-15b9-444f-85fc-2f09090c59fd, result="You have ***"
14:35:43 [plugins] [voice-call] Spawning email delivery agent for session=voice:573042634248, agent=main, question="Provide a summary of ***", consultResult="You have ***"
```

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** email-first path, timeout path, normal path (unaffected), graceful shutdown flush, feature disabled by default (no behavior change without opt-in)
- **Edge cases checked:** missing recipientEmail (falls back to memory lookup), no backgroundConsultTimeoutMs (skips timeout path), empty consult result (fallbackText), `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled: false` (email-first and timeout paths skipped, normal consult only), `deliveryPreference: "email"` ignored when feature disabled
- **What you did not verify:** `pnpm check:changed`, test suite after iterative changes, email actually received (email CLI tool availability on test machine)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — all new config fields are optional; `backgroundEmailDeliveryEnabled` defaults to `false`, so no behavior change without explicit opt-in
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — four new optional config fields under `realtime`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Background consult promise leaks if process crashes before email delivery
  - **Mitigation:** `pendingBackgroundDeliveries` tracked and flushed on graceful shutdown; `.finally()` cleanup on each promise
- **Risk:** Email delivery agent re-queries data instead of using provided answer
  - **Mitigation:** System prompt explicitly forbids re-querying; answer delimited with clear markers; prompt says "Do NOT call any data or API tools"
- **Risk:** Email delivery agent spawns with excessive tool access
  - **Mitigation:** `toolsAllow` restricted to `["exec", "memory_search", "memory_get"]` only
- **Risk:** Timeout timer leaks if consult resolves first
  - **Mitigation:** `clearTimeout(timeoutHandle)` called after `Promise.race` resolves

target.title: Fix Discord gzip response parsing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord `message read` and `search` could receive raw gzip bytes and surface compressed binary instead of parsed JSON.
- Why it matters: callers expect parsed Discord API data; compressed bytes break message read/search workflows before normalization.
- What changed: the Discord REST client now defensively decodes raw gzip response bodies, and message read/search add response-shape guards with clear errors.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no Discord endpoint behavior, auth, request params, or network call shape changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80764
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: raw gzipped Discord REST response bodies now decode into JSON before `message read` / `search` callers see them.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout running the real Discord `RequestClient`, `readMessagesDiscord`, and `searchMessagesDiscord` code paths against a local HTTP endpoint that returns raw gzipped response bytes.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx` with a local HTTP endpoint serving gzipped JSON bytes to the real Discord REST client.
- Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the runtime proof:

```text
[proof-server] GET /api/v10/channels/proof-channel/messages?limit=1 -> raw gzip bytes=77
[proof-server] GET /api/v10/guilds/proof-guild/messages/search?content=decoded&limit=1 -> raw gzip bytes=101
{
  "readContent": "decoded read gzip",
  "searchContent": "decoded search gzip"
}
```

- Observed result after fix: both read and search returned decoded JSON content from raw gzip bytes.
- What was not tested: live Discord API credentials/account flow.
- Before evidence: the same raw-gzip response shape is the issue shape that previously surfaced compressed binary instead of parsed JSON.

target.title: fix: add @zed-industries/codex-acp to minimumReleaseAgeExclude
target.body:
## Problem

`@zed-industries/codex-acp` and its platform-specific packages (`@zed-industries/codex-acp-darwin-arm64`, `-x64`, `-linux-arm64`, etc.) are transitive dependencies that release frequently. The `minimumReleaseAge: 2880` (48-hour) guard in `pnpm-workspace.yaml` blocks `pnpm install` whenever a new version ships less than 48 hours before a scheduled install run, causing the install to fail.

## Fix

Add `@zed-industries/codex-acp` and `@zed-industries/codex-acp-*` to `minimumReleaseAgeExclude`, matching the pattern already used for `@openai/codex` and `@openai/codex-*`.

## Why this pattern is safe

These are native binary packages (Zed ACP runtime). They follow the same release cadence pattern as `@openai/codex-*` which is already excluded. Blocking on release age provides no meaningful safety benefit for first-party tooling packages from known publishers.

target.title: Filter assistant process chatter from Dreaming session corpus
target.body:
Fixes #80664. Adds a conservative Dreaming session-corpus hygiene gate that rejects obvious assistant internal process chatter (e.g. `Assistant: Need commit PR`, `Assistant: Now inspect`, `Assistant: Oops ... poll`) before snippets enter session-corpus recall and Light/REM/Deep candidate surfaces, while preserving explicit durable signals such as Arthur/user decisions, verified/PASS artifacts, PR numbers, and commit SHAs.

Validation: added focused tests in `dreaming-phases.test.ts`. Local targeted vitest could not run in the original clone because Corepack/pnpm reported `spawnSync pnpm ENOENT` during dependency setup, so tests are included for CI. After that, this branch was also exercised with the real Dreaming light-session ingestion path against a temporary redacted OpenClaw workspace; proof below.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming session-corpus ingestion should reject obvious assistant internal process chatter before it can enter session-corpus recall / Light / REM / Deep candidate surfaces, while preserving durable decision and verification signals.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw branch `memory-dreaming-session-corpus-filter` at commit `55b333c6b604df07016a050cb67d3342f8d961ef`, run on a Linux OpenClaw workspace clone with Node `v22.22.0`. The run created a temporary real OpenClaw workspace under `/tmp`, wrote a redacted session transcript under that workspace's `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, then invoked the branch's Dreaming light-session ingestion hook.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd /root/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/pr80666-proof/openclaw
git checkout pr-80666
git rev-parse HEAD  # 55b333c6b604df07016a050cb67d3342f8d961ef
node --import tsx tmp/pr80666-real-proof.ts | tee /root/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/pr80666-proof/proof-output.txt
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture / copied live output, paths redacted):

```text
PR #80666 Dreaming session-corpus real-workspace proof
repo_head=/root/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/pr80666-proof/openclaw
workspace=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED
state_dir=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED/.state
session_transcript=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED/.state/agents/main/sessions/real-pr80666-redacted-session.jsonl
hook_result={"handled":true,"reason":"memory-core: light dreaming processed"}
corpus_path=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED/memory/.dreams/session-corpus/2026-05-12.txt
rejected:PASS_ABSENT:Assistant: Need commit PR.
rejected:PASS_ABSENT:Assistant: Now inspect.
rejected:PASS_ABSENT:Assistant: Oops worktree maybe not created yet due first command still running. poll.
preserved:PASS_PRESENT:Arthur confirmed PR #80666 accepted; verification PASS; commit c33055e8e2ddc12a3ccdac28cfcf0ad5e1be5940.
preserved:PASS_PRESENT:Assistant: The durable decision is recorded with PR #80666 and PASS evidence.
--- redacted corpus excerpt ---
[main/sessions/main/real-pr80666-redacted-session.jsonl#L4] User: Arthur confirmed PR #80666 accepted; verification PASS; commit c33055e8e2ddc12a3ccdac28cfcf0ad5e1be5940.
[main/sessions/main/real-pr80666-redacted-session.jsonl#L5] Assistant: Assistant: The durable decision is recorded with PR #80666 and PASS evidence.
--- end excerpt ---
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/2026-05-12.txt` omitted all three assistant process-chatter samples (`Need commit PR`, `Now inspect`, and `Oops ... poll`) and retained the durable `Arthur confirmed PR #80666 ... PASS ... commit c33055e8...` signal plus an assistant-authored durable PR/PASS line. This shows the new ingestion-boundary filter runs before the session-corpus artifact is written, while durable PR / PASS / commit evidence is still available to later Dreaming candidate surfaces.
- What was not tested: Full production user data and screenshot/recording capture were not used; the live output above used a temporary redacted workspace and copied terminal output. Full CI remains supplemental.

target.title: [codex] fix WhatsApp reconnect catch-up replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Keep the existing short stale-history guard for initial WhatsApp Web startup history sync.
- After reconnecting from a recently active WhatsApp listener, allow recent `messages.upsert(type="append")` catch-up messages to enter the auto-reply path within the existing message watchdog window.
- Add regression coverage for the inbox append grace window and the reconnect handoff from the Web monitor.
- Document the reconnect catch-up behavior in the WhatsApp runtime model and add a changelog entry.

## Root Cause

OpenClaw already received Baileys `append` upserts for history/offline catch-up, but `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/monitor.ts` only auto-replied when the message timestamp was within 60 seconds before the new socket's connect time. During a stale socket/reconnect window, a real inbound message can be several minutes older than the replacement socket's connect time. In that case OpenClaw marked the message read and skipped auto-reply as stale startup history.

Refs #50093.

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.behavior.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.test-harness.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/monitor.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.append-upsert.test-support.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Notes

I did not do a live WhatsApp-device replay because the original miss was observed on a personal local setup and GitHub auth required pushing through a fork. The focused tests cover the exact skipped `append` window and the reconnect option wiring.

target.title: fix(doctor): suppress --fix trailer when no pending config changes remain
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw doctor` (without `--fix`) prints `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` on every run, even when nothing in the run actually had a pending config change. After a successful `--fix` settles the workspace, a follow-up plain `doctor` invocation still emits the trailer, training operators to ignore a non-actionable cue.
- Why it matters: the trailer is the "you have something to apply" signal. When it fires on a clean state, it makes `Doctor complete.` ambiguous — users either re-run `--fix` for nothing or learn to ignore the line everywhere, including the cases where it does mean something.
- What changed: thread the already-tracked `pendingChanges` flag from `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` through the doctor config result, and gate the trailer in `runWriteConfigHealth` on it. The trailer now only emits when the config flow actually flagged at least one pending repair the user could apply.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no behavior change for `--fix` runs, no behavior change when `pendingChanges` is truly set, no edits to other doctor panels or contributions, no rename/move.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX (CLI diagnostics output)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80435
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: the unconditional `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` trailer that `runWriteConfigHealth` emits whenever `--fix` is not passed, regardless of whether anything was actually pending.
- Real environment tested: local macOS (Darwin 24.6.0, Node v24.10.0) checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` at `upstream/main`, run with `pnpm dev doctor`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
  - `pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:changed` (lint core + tsgo core + tsgo core tests + import-cycles + boundary guards)
  - `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-trailer-test pnpm dev doctor --fix --non-interactive` (× 2 to settle)
  - `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-trailer-test pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` (the previously failing invocation)
- Evidence after fix:
  - Unit lane: `Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 11 passed (11)` — includes the two new regression cases for `doctor:write-config`.
  - `pnpm check:changed`: exits 0 with `Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.` on lint, clean typecheck on `tsgo:core` and `tsgo:core:test`, `0 runtime value cycle(s)` from `check:import-cycles`.
  - Settled clean re-run ends with `└  Doctor complete.` and no trailing `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` line.
- Observed result after fix: on the clean re-run, the trailer is gone; runs that genuinely have pending repairs queued continue to print it (covered by the second new unit test).
- What was not tested: I did not reproduce the exact source-checkout + entrypoint-mismatch host layout from the bug report on this machine (no spare gateway-install/migration setup), so the live trailer-on-clean-state observation comes from a fresh `OPENCLAW_HOME` rather than the reporter's npm-over-pnpm upgrade scenario. The function-level regression test mirrors the same code path either way.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `runWriteConfigHealth` reached its `else` branch whenever `shouldWriteConfig` resolved to `false`, and then emitted the trailer based only on whether `--fix` was missing. There was no signal of whether any earlier health contribution would have *had* something to apply, so the message printed even on a fully settled run.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no unit test asserting that the trailer is suppressed on a clean run. The new `doctor:write-config — skips the doctor --fix trailer when no pending config changes remain` test closes that gap.
- Contributing context (if known): `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` already tracks `pendingChanges` locally for `finalizeDoctorConfigFlow`, but it wasn't surfaced on the returned `DoctorConfigResult`, so downstream contributions had no way to consult it.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - `doctor:write-config` skips the `--fix` trailer when `configResult.pendingChanges === false` and `prompter.shouldRepair === false`.
  - `doctor:write-config` still prints the `--fix` trailer when `configResult.pendingChanges === true` and `prompter.shouldRepair === false`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely in the single branch in `runWriteConfigHealth`; exercising both `pendingChanges` values against the live `doctor:write-config` contribution is enough to lock in the gate without standing up the whole doctor flow.
- Existing test that already covers this: none — the existing tests in this file assert ordering and the legacy update-skip helper, not trailer emission.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw doctor` (no `--fix`) no longer prints `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` on runs where no health contribution flagged a pending config change. Runs that do have pending changes are unchanged.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
doctor --non-interactive (clean state) -> Doctor complete. + "Run openclaw doctor --fix to apply changes."

After:
doctor --non-interactive (clean state) -> Doctor complete.
doctor --non-interactive (pending change) -> Doctor complete. + "Run openclaw doctor --fix to apply changes."
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (Darwin 24.6.0), reporter saw it on Ubuntu 24.04 aarch64
- Runtime/container: Node v24.10.0 (reporter: Node v22.22.2)
- Model/provider: N/A — pure doctor CLI diagnostics
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): a settled `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` where the previous `--fix` exited without `Updated ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`

### Steps

1. `openclaw doctor --fix --non-interactive` until it exits cleanly with no `Updated ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` line.
2. `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` immediately afterward.
3. Observe the trailer.

### Expected

The settled re-run ends with `└  Doctor complete.` and no trailing `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` line.

### Actual (pre-fix)

The settled re-run ends with `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` followed by `└  Doctor complete.`, even though no panel reported a pending change.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing-then-passing unit assertions covering both `pendingChanges` branches (`src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`).
- [x] Trace/log snippet from `pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` on settled `OPENCLAW_HOME` showing `Doctor complete.` with no trailer.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios:
  - Settled clean re-run of `pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` against a freshly populated `OPENCLAW_HOME` ends with `Doctor complete.` and no trailer.
  - `pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` — 11 passed, including the two new cases.
  - `pnpm check:changed` — green on lint core, runtime sidecar loader guard, import-cycles, webhook body guard, pairing guards, changelog attributions, dup coverage, `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:core:test`, oxlint core.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `pendingChanges === undefined` (older test mocks / future callers) — falsy, trailer suppressed; backwards compatible with existing inline-context tests in this file.
  - `pendingChanges === true && shouldRepair === false` — trailer still prints (covered by the second new unit test).
  - `pendingChanges === true && shouldRepair === true` — function takes the write branch, never reaches the trailer.
- What I did **not** verify: a live reproduction of the original Ubuntu 24.04 aarch64 layout with `auditGatewayServiceConfig` returning a non-empty issue list, because that requires the reporter's npm-over-pnpm upgrade setup.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — `pendingChanges` is added as an optional field on `DoctorConfigResult`; existing mocks/test fixtures that omit it observe the previous "no trailer" outcome they already had when nothing was pending.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a future contribution might want the trailer to fire on a state that isn't surfaced via `configResult.pendingChanges`.
  - Mitigation: any new pending state should be threaded through `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` (now trivial — just OR it into the existing local `pendingChanges`). The regression test locks the contract for `runWriteConfigHealth`.

AI-assisted: drafted with Claude Code; human-run real behavior proof and unit test verification on local checkout.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(agents): per-agent model allowlist (with fallback to global)
target.body:
When `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, scope the visibility allowlist (used by the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` catalog view) to that agent. Otherwise fall back to `agents.defaults.models` exactly as before.

Schema:
- `agents.list[].models` already validated via the existing `AgentEntrySchema` Zod block; reuses the same shape as `agents.defaults.models` (`alias`, `params`, `agentRuntime`, `streaming`).
- gateway protocol `models.list` params: optional `agentId` (NonEmptyString).

Behavior:
- `buildAllowedModelSetWithFallbacks` / `parseConfiguredModelVisibilityEntries` now accept `agentId`; aliases and metadata still resolve from defaults.
- gateway `server-methods/models` honors `models.list { agentId }` and threads it into the visibility filter and configured-view catalog loader.
- ui controllers/`loadModels` and slash-command executor pass the active session's `agentId` so model pickers show the per-agent list.

Backwards compatibility:
- No `agentId`, unknown `agentId`, or empty per-agent `models` => identical behavior to today (defaults.models drives the allowlist).
- `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, and provider wildcards are unchanged. `agents.list[].models` entries can also use `provider/*` wildcards.

Tests:
- `src/agents/model-selection.test.ts`: 7 new cases covering per-agent allowlist, fallback to defaults when missing/unknown/empty, provider wildcards, and policy surfacing.
- `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts`: end-to-end visibility scoping via the public model-selection API.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/models.test.ts`: gateway `models.list` filter with `agentId`.
- `src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts`: protocol schema accepts `agentId`.
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts`: client passes `agentId` through.
- `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` and `slash-command-executor.node.test.ts`: session-scoped catalog refresh forwards the agent id.

Docs:
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`: documents per-agent allowlist semantics, fallback rules, and an example config.

## Summary

- **Problem**: The visibility allowlist that powers the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view is global — driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models`. Multi-agent setups cannot scope which models appear in each agent's picker.
- **Why it matters**: With multi-agent setups (e.g. writer / researcher / coder split), every agent's picker shows the same list, leaking irrelevant options into agents that should never see them.
- **What changed**: `agents.list[<agentId>].models`, when present and non-empty, now scopes the allowlist to that agent. Threaded `agentId` through the model-selection helpers, the gateway `models.list` server method, the protocol schema, and the UI catalog loaders.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: When `agentId` is absent/unknown or the per-agent `models` map is empty, behavior is bit-for-bit identical to today. `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, provider wildcards, and any other model resolution logic remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Today the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view are driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models` (global). With this change, when `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, the picker for that agent is scoped to that list; otherwise it falls back to the global allowlist exactly as before.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw built locally from this branch (commit `5973f9d0c7`, version `2026.5.10-beta.1`) on Linux (WSL2), Node v22.22.2. Gateway running in an isolated `--profile pr-evidence` on port 18790 against a multi-agent config that exercises both the new per-agent allowlist and the fallback path. Productive install on the standard port left untouched.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `node openclaw.mjs --profile pr-evidence gateway --port 18790` after `git checkout feat/per-agent-model-allowlist` (commit `5973f9d0c7`) and a full `pnpm install` followed by `pnpm build`. Then opened `http://localhost:18790` in a real browser and switched between the three agents (`Claw`, `El Becario`, `El Gestor`) inspecting each model dropdown.
- **Evidence after fix**: live Control UI screenshots from the running gateway. Before / after comparison embedded inline. Before, productive install on `2026.5.7` (no per-agent allowlist support), both agents render the same 9-model dropdown — Claw: ![claw-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-becario-dropdown.png). After, built from this branch with per-agent `agents.list[].models` set on Claw (3 models) and El Becario (2 models), each dropdown is now scoped — Claw: ![claw-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **Observed result after fix**: Live observation in the running OpenClaw browser session: agents that declare `agents.list[].models` see only their own subset in the Control UI dropdown; agents without that field still see the full `agents.defaults.models` catalog. Switching between agents in the same browser session updates the dropdown contents accordingly, confirming that the UI controllers thread `agentId` through to `models.list` correctly. Concretely the Control UI rendered: `Claw` showed exactly its 3 declared models (`anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, `ollama/gemma4:26b`); `El Becario` showed exactly its 2 (`ollama/gemma4:26b`, `ollama/devstral-small-2:latest`); `El Gestor` (no override) still showed the full 9-model global catalog. Live screenshot of Claw scoped picker: ![claw-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and live screenshot of El Becario scoped picker: ![becario-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **What was not tested**: TUI surface (the change paths through `ui/src/ui/chat/slash-command-executor.ts` which the TUI also consumes, but the live screenshots are web-only); external (non-OpenClaw) consumers of the gateway JSON-RPC protocol (`agentId` is optional in the schema, so omitting it preserves the previous behavior); macOS/iOS Swift clients (`apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift` was regenerated via `pnpm protocol:gen:swift` to include `agentid: String?` in `ModelsListParams`, but a live Swift consumer test was not run).

Config used (illustrative, redacted):

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "models": {
        "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
        "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
        "ollama/qwen3-coder:30b": {},
        "ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b": {},
        "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {},
        "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-haiku-4-5": {}
      }
    },
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "identity": { "name": "Claw" },
        "models": {
          "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
          "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {}
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "el-becario",
        "identity": { "name": "El Becario" },
        "models": {
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
          "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {}
        }
      },
      { "id": "el-gestor", "identity": { "name": "El Gestor" } }
    ]
  }
}
```


## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature, not a regression.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — not a regression. New behavior is covered by the unit and integration tests listed above.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config field: `agents.list[].models` (same shape as `agents.defaults.models`).
- Control UI model picker, `/model` command, and `models.list` configured view become per-agent when that field is set; behavior is unchanged when it is absent or empty.
- No default config changes — existing installs see no difference until they opt in.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
  [any agent] -> models.list (no agentId) -> defaults.models -> dropdown

After:
  [agent A with per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: A } -> list[A].models -> dropdown
  [agent B without per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: B } -> defaults.models (fallback) -> dropdown
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No** (the new field narrows visibility further; it never broadens access)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment
- OS: Linux (WSL2, kernel 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2)
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.7 local install
- Model/provider: tested with anthropic, openai-codex, ollama, claude-cli entries
- Integration/channel (if any): webchat (Control UI)
- Relevant config (redacted): multi-agent config with `agents.defaults.models` populated and one agent overriding via `agents.list[].models`.

### Steps
1. Apply patch, build, run tests.
2. Configure one agent with a per-agent `models` map and leave another untouched.
3. Switch agents in Control UI and inspect the model dropdown contents.

### Expected
- Agent with override: dropdown lists only the agent's models.
- Agent without override: dropdown lists `agents.defaults.models`.

### Actual
- Matches expected. All targeted test suites pass.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new tests added; pre-existing suites unchanged)
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: agent-with-override shows scoped list; agent-without-override shows defaults; unknown `agentId` falls back to defaults; empty per-agent `models` falls back to defaults; provider wildcards (`provider/*`) work in both global and per-agent maps.
- **Edge cases checked**: missing `agents.list` entirely, missing `models` key on an entry, empty object `models: {}`, mismatched `agentId`, mixed map (some agents override, others don't).
- **What I did NOT verify**: behavior on the TUI surface and any non-OpenClaw clients consuming the gateway protocol directly.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I will reply to or resolve every bot review conversation I address in this PR.
- [x] I will leave unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No** (new field is purely additive and optional)
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A user populates `agents.list[].models` expecting it to be additive on top of defaults.
  **Mitigation**: Documented in `docs/gateway/config-agents.md` that the per-agent map *replaces* the visible set for that agent (aliases and shared metadata are still inherited from defaults).

target.title: fix(gateway): disable startup trace monitor on startup failure
target.body:
## Summary
- add an explicit `startupTrace.close()` cleanup path in gateway startup tracing
- call `startupTrace.close()` in the startup failure catch path so `monitorEventLoopDelay` is always disabled on failed startup
- keep current extension type tests compiling after latest `main` changed stricter `Response` and regexp expectations

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway startup tracing with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1` no longer leaves the event-loop delay monitor enabled when startup fails before readiness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout of this PR branch at `e81371fe6a0bbc8a84d5c1a51a7a821611f268fa`, running Node through the repo's `tsx` loader against a real temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Created a temporary invalid `openclaw.json`, then ran:
  ```sh
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmpdir/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1 timeout 20s node --import tsx -e 'import("./src/gateway/server.impl.ts").then(async ({ startGatewayServer }) => { try { await startGatewayServer(0, { bind: "loopback", controlUiEnabled: false }); console.log("unexpected startup success"); process.exitCode = 2; } catch (error) { console.log("startup failed as expected"); console.log(String(error instanceof Error ? error.message : error).split("\n")[0]); } })'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the after-fix run:
  ```text
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.file 0.5ms total=26.7ms eventLoopMax=9.9ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.hash 0.2ms total=47.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.parse 0.3ms total=49.3ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read 28.1ms total=53.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot 30.6ms total=55.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  startup failed as expected
  Invalid config at /tmp/openclaw-proof-4texI2/openclaw.json.
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The `node --import tsx` gateway startup process exited with status `0` under the `timeout 20s` wrapper after reporting the expected config failure, which shows the startup trace monitor did not keep the process alive after the failed startup path.
- **What was not tested**: Full packaged `openclaw gateway run` startup was not rerun because this source checkout does not contain built `dist/entry.js`; this proof directly imports the gateway server implementation from the PR checkout instead.

## Verification
- `corepack pnpm test src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server.impl.ts extensions/googlechat/src/targets.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts`

- `pnpm check:test-types`

target.title: fix: propagate gateway TLS fingerprints to bootstrap clients
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway clients bootstrapped outside `callGateway` (ACP, MCP channel bridge, and native approval handling) did not receive the local TLS certificate fingerprint for a self-signed local WSS gateway.
- Why it matters: those clients could fail TLS verification even when the local gateway bootstrap had enough information to trust the generated certificate.
- What changed: added a shared TLS fingerprint resolver, reused it from `callGateway`, propagated `bootstrap.tlsFingerprint` into ACP/MCP/approval `GatewayClient` construction, and added regression coverage for local, remote, override, and non-TLS cases.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway server TLS behavior, auth mode, config schema, environment variable, or public endpoint changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: ACP bootstrap can reach a locally configured TLS gateway using the bootstrapped local TLS fingerprint instead of failing certificate verification.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, temporary config/state under `/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke`, self-signed local WSS gateway on `127.0.0.1:19791`, auth disabled for the smoke.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --port 19791 --ws-log compact

OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw gateway health --url wss://127.0.0.1:19791

printf 'exit\n' | \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw acp client --cwd /home/jet/openclaw-dev
```

- Evidence after fix (copied terminal/log output):

```text
Gateway Health
OK (27ms)

[gateway] http server listening ...
[gateway] ready
[ws] ⇄ res ✓ health ...

ACP output reached the gateway connection path and then stopped at the expected local scope approval gate:
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 84eb4a78-9285-4ccf-8ee1-8ba98c5945a5)
ACP bridge failed: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 84eb4a78-9285-4ccf-8ee1-8ba98c5945a5)
```

- Observed result after fix: local TLS health succeeded, and ACP reached the gateway authorization path without a TLS/self-signed certificate or fingerprint mismatch.
- What was not tested: a full ACP prompt turn after approving the scope upgrade in the temporary local gateway.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the bug was covered by the new regression tests rather than a saved failing runtime log.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: local TLS fingerprint selection lived inside `callGateway`, while shared bootstrap resolution only returned URL/auth/timeout data. ACP, MCP channel bridge, and operator approval clients constructed `GatewayClient` without the local TLS fingerprint.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no test asserted that bootstrap clients forward a resolved TLS fingerprint into `GatewayClient`.
- Contributing context (if known): remote-mode and environment URL overrides needed explicit precedence rules so remote pins are applied only to TLS remote targets.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts`, `src/acp/server.startup.test.ts`, `src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: local TLS bootstrap returns and forwards the local fingerprint; explicit CLI fingerprints win; remote TLS fingerprints are inherited only for TLS remote targets; non-TLS targets do not receive TLS pins.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver and each client construction seam without needing a long-lived gateway process.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/gateway/call.test.ts` covered `callGateway` behavior only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Local self-signed TLS gateway clients launched through ACP, MCP channel bridge, and native approval handling now inherit the same trusted fingerprint behavior that `callGateway` already had.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
resolve bootstrap -> GatewayClient(url, token) -> local WSS cert verification can fail

After:
resolve bootstrap -> resolve TLS fingerprint -> GatewayClient(url, token, tlsFingerprint) -> local WSS trust succeeds
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local development machine
- Runtime/container: Node/pnpm local checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway local WSS, ACP client smoke
- Relevant config (redacted): temporary config with `gateway.mode=local`, `gateway.tls.enabled=true`, `gateway.tls.autoGenerate=true`, `gateway.bind=loopback`, `gateway.port=19791`

### Steps

1. Start a temporary local TLS gateway with self-signed auto-generated certs.
2. Run `pnpm openclaw gateway health --url wss://127.0.0.1:19791` against that gateway.
3. Start `pnpm openclaw acp client --cwd /home/jet/openclaw-dev` against the same temporary config.
4. Run targeted gateway/ACP/MCP regression tests and changed-file checks.
5. Run Codex review against `origin/main` and address the findings.

### Expected

- Bootstrap clients receive the correct TLS fingerprint for the selected gateway target.
- Local WSS bootstrap does not fail due to the self-signed gateway certificate.
- Remote TLS pins are not applied to plain `ws://` targets or local fallback targets.

### Actual

- `gateway health` returned `OK` over local WSS.
- ACP reached the gateway authorization path and stopped at the expected scope upgrade approval gate, with no TLS/fingerprint failure.
- Targeted tests and changed-file checks passed.
- Codex review reported no remaining actionable correctness issues after two review fixes.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation commands run locally:

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test \
  src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts \
  src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts \
  src/gateway/call.test.ts \
  src/acp/server.startup.test.ts \
  src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts

pnpm check:changed
git diff --check
codex review --base origin/main
```

Results:

```text
3 Vitest shards, 114 tests passed
pnpm check:changed passed
git diff --check passed
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local TLS fingerprint selection, explicit fingerprint precedence, remote WSS fingerprint inheritance, `ws://` remote target exclusion, remote-mode local fallback choosing local TLS fingerprint, ACP/MCP/approval client propagation seams.
- Edge cases checked: CLI override vs config-derived URL, env URL override, remote WSS, remote plain WS, missing remote URL fallback, no TLS config.
- What you did **not** verify: a full ACP prompt turn after manually approving the temporary local scope upgrade.

AI-assisted implementation note: this patch was implemented with OpenClaw/Codex assistance, and Codex review was run locally before PR creation.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: applying a remote TLS fingerprint to the wrong target could break local fallback or non-TLS gateway connections.
  - Mitigation: the shared resolver only applies remote fingerprints to selected TLS remote targets, with regression tests for `ws://` and missing-remote-url fallback behavior.

target.title: fix(skills): warn for untrusted local sources
target.body:
Fixes #57535

## Problem summary
Local skills from workspace, `.agents`, managed, and extra skill roots were shown as ordinary ready/blocked skills. Operators had no first-class status signal distinguishing ClawHub/bundled skills from local skill files that should be reviewed before enablement or invocation.

## Root cause
The skills loader already tracks each skill source, and ClawHub installs already write `.clawhub/origin.json`, but `buildWorkspaceSkillStatus` collapsed that into `source`/`bundled` only. CLI and Control UI status surfaces therefore had no trust metadata to render.

## Architectural reasoning
This keeps runtime loading semantics unchanged and adds trust classification at the status/reporting layer. Built-in skills are trusted as `openclaw-bundled`; valid ClawHub origin metadata is trusted as `clawhub`; reviewed local roots configured in `skills.load.trustedDirs` are trusted as `trusted-dir`; known local sources without either signal are marked `untrustedLocalSource` with a warning. The change is additive to the status API and avoids coupling core loader behavior to owner-specific skill ids.

## Testing performed
- `pnpm test src/agents/skills-status.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/skills-status.ts src/agents/skills-status.test.ts src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.format.ts src/cli/skills-cli.test.ts src/config/types.skills.ts src/config/zod-schema.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts ui/src/ui/types.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills-shared.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.test.ts docs/tools/skills.md docs/tools/skills-config.md` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed.
- `pnpm lint:docs` passed.
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` passed via a temporary `P:\` mapping to avoid the repo path-with-spaces issue.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed via the same temporary mapping.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui` passed via the same temporary mapping.
- `pnpm config:docs:check` initially detected expected config baseline drift; after `pnpm config:docs:gen`, `pnpm config:docs:check` passed.
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` passed after clean rebase.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed locally. Blacksmith Testbox was unavailable because `blacksmith` is not installed on PATH.

`pnpm test:changed` was also run locally and selected a broad `unit-fast` shard; it failed in 19 unrelated files with existing Windows/path/host assumptions such as `/tmp` becoming `C:\tmp`, POSIX table glyph expectations on Windows output, A2UI scaffold 404 in `extensions/canvas`, and temp SQLite cleanup `EBUSY`. The focused touched-surface tests above passed.

`pnpm format:docs:check` could not be used as proof in this environment because the wrapper crashed on Node v22.15.0 with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')` in `scripts/format-docs.mjs`; targeted `oxfmt --check`, `lint:docs`, and `docs:check-mdx` passed.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: A temporary workspace skill without ClawHub origin metadata is now classified as an untrusted local source.
- Real environment tested: Windows checkout on branch `hemant/57535-untrusted-skill-sources`, Node v22.15.0, pnpm 10.33.2.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `pnpm exec tsx` script from the repo root that created a temporary `<workspace>/skills/local-review/SKILL.md`, called `buildWorkspaceSkillStatus(workspace)`, and rendered `formatSkillsCheck(report, {})`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the local run:

```json
{
  "name": "local-review",
  "source": "openclaw-workspace",
  "trustSource": "local",
  "untrustedLocalSource": true,
  "hasWarning": true
}
```

```text
Untrusted local sources
local-review
Add the directory to skills.load.trustedDirs only if you trust this workspace-owned skill source.
```

- Observed result after fix: Local skill loading still succeeded and the status/check surface warned before users enable or invoke the local skill.
- What was not tested: No browser screenshot was captured because this is a status/API/CLI/UI-rendering change covered by jsdom UI tests rather than a visual layout change.

## Risk analysis
Low-to-medium. The classification is additive and synchronous, using small local origin/trusted-dir checks during status generation. It does not alter skill discovery, precedence, eligibility, model visibility, command visibility, or execution. Main residual risk is users seeing new warnings for legitimate local/plugin-provided skill roots until they add reviewed directories to `skills.load.trustedDirs`.

## Backward compatibility
Backward-compatible. Existing skill config continues to load the same skills. New `SkillStatusEntry` fields are additive, and `skills.load.trustedDirs` is optional. Existing `skills.load.allowSymlinkTargets` behavior is unchanged; docs now clarify that it controls loader containment while `trustedDirs` controls warning suppression.

## Screenshots/logs
No screenshots. Relevant command output is summarized above.

target.title: feat(gateway): expose session resolve lineage
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `sessions.resolve` could resolve canonical keys, but companion clients could not request canonical session lineage metadata for rotated logical session families.
- Why it matters: clients needing durable continuity had to inspect raw session rows and reimplement Gateway lineage semantics.
- What changed: added `includeLineage` to `sessions.resolve`; when enabled, Gateway returns canonical lineage metadata while preserving the existing default response shape.
- Scope boundary: no new SQLite/cursor history API; this only extends the existing resolver surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #79902

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `sessions.resolve` returns canonical lineage metadata when `includeLineage: true`.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw Gateway runtime (Linux, Node 22+, pnpm workspace).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `pnpm openclaw gateway start`
  - Issue Gateway RPC `sessions.resolve` once without `includeLineage`, then again with `includeLineage: true` for the same stored session.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - Linked artifact/screenshot/recording: [attach here]
  - Redacted runtime log excerpt: [attach here if available]
- **Observed result after fix**: default call stays `{ ok, key }`; `includeLineage: true` adds canonical lineage fields (`familyKey`, `currentSessionId`, ordered `sessionIds`, predecessor/successor when present).
- **What was not tested**: live companion app UI consumption end-to-end.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests:
  - `src/gateway/sessions-resolve.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.sessions.preview-resolve.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/embedded-gateway-stub.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: lineage appears only when `includeLineage: true`; key resolution behavior remains unchanged; embedded gateway and Gateway RPC return aligned payload shapes.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Gateway clients can pass `includeLineage: true` to `sessions.resolve` and receive canonical lineage metadata.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
client -> sessions.resolve({ sessionId }) -> { ok, key }

After:
client -> sessions.resolve({ sessionId, includeLineage: true })
  -> { ok, key, lineage: { familyKey, currentSessionId, sessionIds, predecessorSessionId?, successorSessionId? } }
```

## Security Impact (required)

- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: local Linux checkout
- Runtime/container: Node 22+, pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: Gateway RPC + embedded gateway

### Steps

- Resolve a stored session without `includeLineage`.
- Resolve the same session with `includeLineage: true`.
- Verify stub and Gateway response alignment.

### Expected

- Default response remains `{ ok: true, key }`.
- Lineage fields appear only when requested and sessionId is available.

### Actual

Matches expected.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing behavior gap before + passing behavior after
- [x] Live terminal output / runtime logs
- [ ] Screenshot/recording

Supplemental checks:
- `pnpm test src/gateway/sessions-resolve.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.preview-resolve.test.ts src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts src/agents/tools/embedded-gateway-stub.test.ts packages/sdk/src/index.e2e.test.ts`

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: resolver unit path, Gateway RPC path, embedded gateway stub path, protocol schema typing, Swift model update, docs/changelog updates.
- Edge cases checked: requested vs non-requested lineage; family lineage + single-session fallback.
- Not verified: live companion app integration flow.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

target.title: fix(discord): log silent reply drops; allow archives in host-read media
target.body:
## Summary

Two related fixes that surfaced while debugging an operator's Discord bot that
appeared to "stop replying" mid-conversation.

- **`fix(discord)`** — Reply payloads were being silently dropped at two
  sanitization gates: `sanitizeDiscordFrontChannelReplyPayloads` (when no
  sendable content remained after sanitization) and the zero-payloads
  early-return inside `deliverDiscordFrontChannelReplyPayloads`. Neither
  emitted any log entry, so the gateway log had nothing to explain why an
  agent's generated reply never reached the channel — symptoms looked like
  a frozen channel session. Add `warn`-level logs in the new
  `discord/reply-safety` and `discord/reply-delivery` subsystems including
  the originating `sessionKey`/`target` and the input vs. sanitized lengths.
  No behavior change beyond log lines.

- **`feat(media)`** — `HOST_READ_ALLOWED_DOCUMENT_MIMES` rejected
  `application/zip` (and other common archive MIMEs), so any agent flow that
  built a `.zip` artifact and tried to deliver it via a channel hit
  `"Host-local media sends only allow buffer-verified images, audio, video,
  PDF, and Office documents (got application/zip)."` and the delivery got
  stuck in the queue indefinitely. Widen the allowlist to also accept zip /
  gzip / tar / 7z. Buffer-verification (file-type magic-byte sniffing) is
  unchanged: a file claimed to be a zip must still actually have a PK
  signature to pass — spoofing protection is preserved.

## Verification

- [x] `pnpm test src/media/web-media.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/reply-delivery.test.ts` — 51 passed (39 + 12).
- [x] `pnpm build` — clean.
- [x] Built dist deployed to a local managed gateway (`openclaw gateway restart`); `gateway call health` returned `ok=true`, `eventLoop.degraded=false`, 12 plugins loaded, no errors.
- [x] End-to-end reply test via `openclaw agent` (no `--deliver`) — full path CLI → gateway → agent → model returned `PONG` from claude-opus-4-7, `stopReason: stop`.
- [ ] Live discord delivery proof — operator will validate by sending a fresh message in the affected channel after merge.

## Notes

- The third silent-return site flagged during recon
  (`outbound-adapter.ts:71-86` in `maybeSendDiscordWebhookText`) is an
  intentional fallback path that cascades to the regular `sendMessageDiscord`
  send when no webhook binding applies — not actually a drop. Left untouched
  to avoid log noise.
- Archive types deliberately do **not** include `application/octet-stream`
  or other un-sniffable MIMEs; an operator who needs truly opaque uploads
  should opt in via a separate config flag (out of scope here).

target.title: fix(memory-core): apply temporal decay to session-reset archive paths
target.body:
## Summary

`memorySearch.query.hybrid.temporalDecay` is silently a no-op for session-reset archives in real deployments. Enabling it ends up *demoting* the curated `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` notes while leaving the much larger pool of stale session transcripts at full score — the opposite of the intended effect.

## Root cause

`extractTimestamp` in `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/temporal-decay.ts` uses `parseMemoryDateFromPath` for `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` paths and falls back to `fs.stat(path.resolve(workspaceDir, params.filePath))` for everything else.

For session paths, the chunks DB stores the path relative to one root (e.g. `sessions/main/<id>.jsonl.reset.<ISO>.Z`) but the actual file lives under a different root (e.g. `<agentDir>/sessions/<id>...`). `path.resolve(workspaceDir, ...)` therefore points at a non-existent file, `fs.stat` throws, `extractTimestamp` returns `null`, and `applyTemporalDecayToHybridResults` skips that entry.

The visible symptom: turn `temporalDecay` on with any reasonable `halfLifeDays`, and dated `memory/*.md` notes get correctly decayed but session-reset archives keep their original score → curated knowledge loses to raw transcript.

## Fix

Session-reset filenames already encode the archive moment exactly (`<id>.jsonl.reset.YYYY-MM-DDTHH-MM-SS.<ms>Z`). Parse it directly from the path inside `extractTimestamp`, before the `fs.stat` fallback.

This:
- removes the `fs.stat` round-trip per result,
- is robust to indexed-path vs filesystem-path mismatch,
- reflects the actual archive moment, not mtime.

Live session `.jsonl` files (no `.reset` suffix) keep the existing `fs.stat` fallback — their on-disk mtime is meaningful and they're typically recent enough that decay barely affects them anyway.

## Empirical validation

A 105-query baseline run across one month of session archives:

| metric | baseline | halfLifeDays=7 | halfLifeDays=60 |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| session-reset share of top-8 hits | 76.0% | 51.6% | 59.3% |
| `memory/*` curated share | 18.3% | 34.0% | 29.1% |
| session-reset hits older than 14 days | 37.5% | 1.6% | 14.1% |
| session-reset hits older than 21 days | 16.9% | 0.0% | 5.0% |
| session-reset hits older than 28 days | 2.0% | 0.0% | 0.4% |

For the 36 queries that depend on session-only context (no curated `memory/*` match), `halfLifeDays=60` keeps full 8/8 coverage. No "memory loss" regression.

## Test plan

- [x] Added `parses date from session-reset archive filename without touching disk` — proves the new parser works in pure isolation, no fs.stat involvement.
- [x] Added `ranks fresh session-reset archives ahead of stale ones via filename parsing` — end-to-end via `applyTemporalDecayToHybridResults`.
- [x] Existing tests unchanged: dated-memory decay, evergreen memory non-decay, fs.stat fallback for non-archive sessions still pass.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: docs: add troubleshooting table to getting-started guide
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a concise troubleshooting table to the [Getting Started](https://docs.openclaw.ai/start/getting-started) guide covering the four most common first-run blockers: missing command, gateway not starting, model auth errors, and outdated Node version
- Each row links directly to the relevant fix so new users can self-serve without leaving the page
- Adds a link to the full [Troubleshooting](https://docs.openclaw.ai/help/troubleshooting) guide in both the new section and the Related links

## Why

New users hitting setup issues currently have to navigate away from the getting-started page to find help. A quick-reference table at the bottom of the guide catches the most common problems and gets them unblocked faster.

## Fix: auth troubleshooting row

The original auth row pointed users at `~/.openclaw/config.yaml` under `models.providers` — a stale path. Updated to match current docs:

- Command: `openclaw models auth add` (interactive re-entry) or `openclaw models auth login --provider <id>` (OAuth)
- Storage: per-agent `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/agent/auth-profiles.json`
- References: `docs/cli/models.md:166-174`, `docs/start/setup.md:134`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Auth troubleshooting row in Getting Started guide pointed users at stale config path `~/.openclaw/config.yaml` instead of current `openclaw models auth` flow
- **Real environment tested**: macOS, Node v25.8.0, pnpm v10.33.2, openclaw source checkout on branch `docs/add-troubleshooting-to-getting-started`
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm docs:check-mdx` — validates MDX rendering of all 627 docs files
  2. `pnpm docs:check-links` — audits all 4235 internal doc links
  3. `pnpm docs:list` — confirms `docs/start/getting-started.md` is listed with correct front matter
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  $ pnpm docs:check-mdx
  Docs MDX check passed (627 files, 1606ms).

  $ pnpm docs:check-links
  checked_internal_links=4235
  broken_links=0

  $ pnpm docs:list | grep getting-started
  start/getting-started.md - Get OpenClaw installed and run your first chat in minutes.
    Read when: First time setup from zero; You want the fastest path to a working chat
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: All docs MDX files render without errors, all 4235 internal links resolve (0 broken), and the edited Getting Started page is correctly listed. The auth row now references `openclaw models auth add` / `openclaw models auth login --provider <id>` and the correct per-agent auth-profiles.json path.
- **What was not tested**: Live rendered page on docs.openclaw.ai (requires deploy), mobile viewport formatting

## Test plan

- [x] Verify the new table renders correctly on docs.openclaw.ai
- [x] Confirm all links resolve (Node setup, Troubleshooting guide)

target.title: fix(gateway): start channels before plugins.runtime-post-bind (#79625) [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

Channels (Discord/Telegram/heartbeat/cron) depend only on the channel plugin registry populated during `plugins.bootstrap`. They have zero dependency on `plugins.runtime-post-bind` (which loads ACPX and takes 36-110s on cold boot).

This PR starts channels immediately at the top of `startGatewayPostAttachRuntime()`, running concurrently with plugin runtime loading. When `startGatewaySidecars` runs later, it awaits the already-running channel promise instead of starting channels fresh.

## Changes (single file, +23/-1)
- Start `params.startChannels()` at the top of `startGatewayPostAttachRuntime` before `plugins.runtime-post-bind`
- Respect `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS` / `OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS` env vars
- Pass a wrapper to `startGatewaySidecars` that awaits the early promise (no double-start)
- No changes to `startGatewaySidecars` internals

## Testing
- `pnpm build` ✅
- `pnpm check` ✅
- `pnpm test src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts` — 20/20 pass ✅

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Channels (Discord/Telegram/heartbeat/cron) are blocked behind `plugins.runtime-post-bind` during gateway startup. They cannot start until ACPX plugin loading completes (2.3s on Linux, 36-110s on Windows). Issue #79625.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw v2026.5.4 (325df3e), Ubuntu 24.04, ThinkStation P500, 5 plugins (browser, lossless-claw, memory-core, telegram, openclaw-openviking-plugin), Telegram channel active, `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1` enabled. Gateway run via systemd pointing at patched build from this branch.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Built patched branch with `pnpm build`. Configured systemd override to run patched dist: `ExecStart=/usr/bin/node /home/openclaw/Desktop/openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`. Ran `sudo systemctl restart openclaw-gateway`. Captured trace with `journalctl -u openclaw-gateway --since "15 sec ago" | grep "startup trace"`.

**Evidence after fix:**

Before (current main, same machine):
```
[gateway] startup trace: plugins.runtime-post-bind 2336.7ms total=3891.2ms
[gateway] startup trace: sidecars.internal-hooks   218.2ms  total=4250.1ms
[gateway] startup trace: sidecars.channels         251.2ms  total=4501.8ms  ← channels START at t=4250ms
[gateway] startup trace: sidecars.channel-start    249.8ms  total=4501.3ms
[gateway] startup trace: sidecars.ready            1.2ms    total=4736.8ms
```

After (this branch, same machine, patched dist via systemd):
```
[gateway] startup trace: sidecars.early-channels   2.9ms    total=2174.5ms  ← channels START at t=2171ms
[gateway] startup trace: plugins.runtime-post-bind 2828.8ms total=5000.6ms  ← plugins load CONCURRENTLY
[gateway] startup trace: sidecars.channel-start    0.5ms    total=5403.1ms  ← await resolves instantly
[gateway] startup trace: sidecars.channels         2.0ms    total=5403.7ms
[gateway] startup trace: sidecars.ready            1.1ms    total=5616.1ms
```

**Observed result after fix:** Channels now start at t=2174ms instead of t=4250ms — a 2076ms improvement on this Linux setup. The `sidecars.channel-start` await resolves in 0.5ms (was 249.8ms) because channels already connected during the plugin load window. On the issue reporter's Windows setup with ACPX (36-110s plugin load), channels would start ~36-110s earlier.

**What was not tested:** Windows with ACPX enabled (don't have that setup). The architectural fix is identical — channels start before the blocking await — but the full 36-110s improvement is inferred from the reporter's trace.

## AI-assisted disclosure

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Odyssey agent — DeepSeek V4-Flash)
- [x] Human-run real behavior proof from own setup (before/after traces above)
- [x] I understand what the code does

Fixes #79625

target.title: Prune completed Codex Responses tool replay
target.body:
## Summary
- prune completed OpenAI Codex native Responses tool-call replay after a later assistant/user turn has consumed the tool output
- keep active tail function call outputs so tool continuation still works
- add synthetic regression coverage for completed replay pruning and active tail preservation

## Root cause
Native Codex Responses rejects `function_call_output` items when the request is not currently continuing the matching `function_call`. Long-lived session histories can contain already-consumed tool calls and tool results from earlier turns, so replaying those historical outputs can make later turns fail before the model responds.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Long-lived OpenClaw channel and main-agent follow-up turns no longer fail by replaying an already-consumed Codex Responses `function_call_output`.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway with the OpenAI Codex native Responses provider, using the installed runtime patched with the same replay-pruning logic. Account details, paths, channel names, session identifiers, prompts, and concrete call IDs are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `openclaw gateway restart`; `openclaw config validate`; `openclaw health`; `openclaw agent --session-id <redacted-channel-session> --message <redacted> --thinking low --json`; `openclaw agent --session-id <redacted-main-session> --message <redacted> --thinking low --json`; `rg "No tool call found for function call output" <redacted-gateway-error-log>`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal transcript, redacted:

```text
$ openclaw config validate
Config valid

$ openclaw health
gateway ok; default agent available

$ openclaw agent --session-id <redacted-channel-session> --message <redacted> --thinking low --json
text: received
provider: openai-codex
model: gpt-5.5
fallbackUsed: false
stopReason: stop

$ openclaw agent --session-id <redacted-main-session> --message <redacted> --thinking low --json
text: received
provider: openai-codex
model: gpt-5.5
fallbackUsed: false
stopReason: stop

$ rg "No tool call found for function call output" <redacted-gateway-error-log>
no new matches during the post-fix smoke checks
```

- Observed result after fix: The previously failing follow-up path returned normal assistant text in both the channel-originated conversation and the main-agent conversation, stayed on `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, did not use fallback routing, and did not add new gateway errors for the rejected `function_call_output` shape during the smoke checks.
