target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. The live staging proof above remains valid: it unbound via the generic `Failed to spawn agent command` failure plus the missing-cwd probe, which is exactly the path this rebased head preserves.

target.title: fix(cron): prevent scheduler death when startup catch-up fails
target.body:
## Summary

When runMissedJobs() throws during start(), the timer was never armed, silently killing the cron scheduler until the next gateway restart.

## Root Cause

The start() function in src/cron/service/ops.ts calls runMissedJobs() outside of any try/catch, followed by the locked() block that calls armTimer(state). If runMissedJobs() throws (e.g. an isolated agent turn fails during startup catch-up), execution never reaches armTimer(), and the scheduler is permanently dead.

## Changes

1. **Guard armTimer in start()** - Wrap runMissedJobs() in try/catch so the timer is always armed
2. **Zombie detection in onTimer()** - Clear stale runningAtMs markers at runtime (threshold: 2x job timeout or 2 min) so jobs recover without restart
3. **Tests** - Verify timer stays armed on catch-up failure and stale markers are cleared

Fixes #67854
Related: #59056, #60799

target.title: Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save
target.body:
# OpenClaw session-store path fix, 2026-04-12

## Status
Applied live to installed runtime. Also ported carefully into a fresh source clone of the matching `v2026.4.11` upstream tag, but not yet merged upstream or installed from that source tree.

## Source clone
- Repo: `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/external/openclaw-upstream`
- Branch: `fix/session-store-transcript-path-canonicalization`

## Protected file
`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/store-Cw5RrVCw.js`

## Backup
`/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/backups/runtime-patches/store-Cw5RrVCw.js.pre-20260412-1233`

## What changed
Added a save-time canonicalization step so persisted `sessionFile` and `transcriptPath` values are rewritten into the real sessions directory before session-store writes hit disk.

## Why
Without this, restored/recovered session entries could keep workspace-style transcript paths, then later be treated as missing/orphaned and get swept or misread.

## Verification performed
- syntax check passed
- synthetic save test passed
- gateway restarted cleanly
- post-restart audit:
  - `entries 913`
  - `legacy_non_agent_keys 0`
  - `non_cron_duplicate_sessionids 0`
  - `missing_jsonl_paths 0`

## Release blocker rule
Treat this as a release blocker until the fix is confirmed upstream and in the installed version. Before any OpenClaw update or manual overwrite touching runtime/config/session storage, stop and verify this protection first.

## Source patch details
Changed files in the source clone:
- `src/config/sessions/store.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`

Validation in source clone:
- `node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`
- Result: `2 passed`, `29 passed`

## Patch export
- Mailbox-style patch export written for handoff/cherry-pick use:
  - `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/patches/openclaw-session-store-path-fix-2026-04-12.patch`

## Current git state
- Branch is clean and still anchored on tag base `v2026.4.11`
- Head commit:
  - `e1c0d1a` `Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save`

## Next upstream action
Open or stage an upstream PR from the source clone using commit `e1c0d1a` or the exported patch above. Keep the live runtime bandage in place until an official release is installed and re-verified on this machine.

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: fix(agent): surface non_deliverable_terminal_turn with specific message and auto-retry
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When a model turn ends with `stop_reason: tool_use` but no explicit terminal delivery, the runtime classifies it as `non_deliverable_terminal_turn`. This `terminalError` was only recorded in trajectory metadata and never surfaced to the reply layer. Users saw the generic "Something went wrong… use /new" message, which gives misleading remediation advice and hides the actual failure.

## Why This Change Was Made

Three complementary changes:

1. **Propagate `trajectoryTerminalError`** from attempt trajectory through `EmbeddedAgentRunMeta` so the reply layer can see the terminal error reason
2. **Auto-retry once** — the model produced no output, so retry is idempotent (same pattern as `consumeTransientHttpRetry`)
3. **Surface a specific, actionable message** when retry is exhausted

## User Impact

- Users see an actionable message instead of generic "Something went wrong… use /new"
- Intermittent `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` errors self-recover via auto-retry
- `/new` is no longer the primary remediation for this class of failure

## Files changed

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — add `trajectoryTerminalError` to return
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/types.ts` — add type field
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/types.ts` — add to `EmbeddedAgentRunMeta`
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts` — propagate through meta
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-failure-copy.ts` — add user-facing message constant
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` — auto-retry + specific message
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` — 3 new tests

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` (stop_reason=tool_use, no delivery) surfaced as generic "Something went wrong… use /new" with no diagnostic signal and no retry.

Real environment tested: Linux x86_64, Node v22.19.0, OpenClaw branch fix/non-deliverable-terminal-turn-message (base d1ab308f5c).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

1. Clean build: `rm -rf dist && pnpm build` — exit 0, no compile errors
2. Dist bundle verification — new code confirmed in 3 production bundles:
   - `dist/agent-runner-failure-copy-BZSo8SgJ.js`: `NON_DELIVERABLE_TERMINAL_TURN_USER_TEXT` = "⚠️ The model stopped between tool calls…"
   - `dist/reply-usage-state-D-I1Ghsz.js`: `nonDeliverableTurnRetriesRemaining`, `consumeNonDeliverableTurnRetry`, log: "Non-deliverable terminal turn detected, retrying once."
   - `dist/selection-BxHKv2QU.js`: `NON_DELIVERABLE_TERMINAL_TURN_REASON` = "non_deliverable_terminal_turn"
3. Gateway startup: `pnpm gateway:watch:raw` — configuration loaded successfully, no crash, no panic, no error
4. Unit tests: 260 tests across 4 test files passed (attempt-trajectory-status 19, agent-runner-execution 207, result-fallback-classifier, trajectory/export)
5. System gateway operational with patched branch: `pnpm openclaw status` confirms gateway running, channel icenter ON/SETUP, sessions active

Evidence after fix:
- `trajectoryTerminalError` propagation chain: resolveAttemptTrajectoryTerminal → attempt.ts return → run.ts meta → EmbeddedAgentRunMeta → reply layer. All 4 links verified in source and dist
- Auto-retry: `consumeNonDeliverableTurnRetry()` with 1-retry budget follows same pattern as `consumeTransientHttpRetry`. Verified in dist bundle.
- Specific message: `NON_DELIVERABLE_TERMINAL_TURN_USER_TEXT` replaces generic `GENERIC_EXTERNAL_RUN_FAILURE_TEXT` when trajectoryTerminalError is set. Verified in dist bundle.
- 3 new unit tests pass: auto-retry triggers, exhausted retry surfaces specific message, fallback-exhausted skips retry

Observed result after fix:

- Gateway starts without errors from patched code

- All 3 code layers verified present in production dist bundles
- When trajectoryTerminalError === "non_deliverable_terminal_turn" with empty payloads, reply layer auto-retries once, then surfaces actionable message

What was not tested: Full end-to-end Telegram DM reproduction under heavy cacheRead load. Requires live API keys + sustained provider load to trigger the race between stop_reason=tool_use and turn closure. The dist verification + gateway startup + 260 tests provide production artifact-level proof that the code paths are correct.

target.title: CI: report circular dependencies in PRs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: We did not have a repo-standard way to detect and inspect runtime circular imports across `src`, `extensions`, and `scripts`.
- Why it matters: These cycles can drag large strongly connected components into the runtime graph and make lazy/runtime boundaries harder to preserve.
- What changed: I added `dependency-cruiser` repo tooling, a focused circular-dependency config, a local report script, and a non-blocking CI job that publishes PR summaries plus raw artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not start failing CI on existing cycles and I did not refactor any of the current cycle clusters in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: This is tooling/CI wiring, so the direct guardrail is running the report script and validating the emitted artifacts/summary locally.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: The value here is report generation rather than product behavior. I verified the report output directly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

PRs now get a non-blocking `dependency-circular-report` job that publishes:

- a summary table of cycle-heavy scopes
- a sorted descending directory/module list
- a Mermaid visualization for scope distribution and largest SCC clusters
- uploaded artifacts with raw JSON and full cycle listings

Local development also gets:

- `pnpm deps:circular`
- `pnpm deps:circular:json`
- `pnpm deps:circular:report`

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR] -> [standard CI lanes only] -> [no circular-import visibility]

After:
[PR] -> [dependency-circular-report job] -> [step summary + artifacts]
     -> [sorted scopes/directories + SCC graphs]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: CI now runs `dependency-cruiser` against repo sources to generate reports. This is limited to checked-in code in the Actions workspace and does not expand runtime privileges beyond normal repo CI tooling.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.8.1 local shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default repo config

### Steps

1. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular`
2. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular:report`
3. Inspect `.artifacts/dependency-cruiser/step-summary.md`, `report.md`, `summary.json`, and `cycle-violations.txt`

### Expected

- `dependency-cruiser` finds current runtime cycles using the repo config
- the report script emits a readable sorted summary plus raw artifacts
- CI can reuse the same script to publish a non-blocking PR report

### Actual

- Local runs completed and emitted the expected artifact set
- Current snapshot found `150` cycle violations, `24` cyclic SCCs, and `195` modules participating in cycles
- Top cycle-heavy scopes were `extensions/browser`, `src/agents`, `extensions/mattermost`, `extensions/zalouser`, and `src/cli`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: I ran the circular check locally, ran the report generator locally, and inspected the generated markdown/JSON/text artifacts for sorting, counts, and representative SCC output.
- Edge cases checked: I confirmed type-only imports are excluded, test files are excluded, the report groups by scope and exact directory, and the summary stays non-blocking.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not yet verify the rendered GitHub Actions step summary or artifact download UX inside a live PR run before opening this PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The report could be noisy or too broad for reviewers if the summary is not distilled enough.
  - Mitigation: The PR summary shows only top scopes/directories/SCCs, while the full raw listing stays in artifacts.
- Risk: The report job adds CI time.
  - Mitigation: It is non-blocking, uses a focused config, and runs in the same lightweight Node environment as other CI checks.

target.title: docs(memory): add memory system deep dive guide
target.body:
## Summary

Add a comprehensive memory system deep dive guide, addressing #43032.

### Problem

`docs/concepts/memory.md` covers basics (~600 words) but lacks depth on memory best practices, search optimization, lifecycle management, and advanced patterns.

### New document: `docs/concepts/memory-deep-dive.md` (401 lines)

Covers all 6 sections requested in the issue:

1. **Memory Architecture** — Two-layer model (`MEMORY.md` + `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`), indexing scope, what is searchable vs not
2. **Writing Effective Memory** — What belongs in MEMORY.md vs daily logs, promotion workflow, formatting tips for better search indexing
3. **Search Optimization** — Hybrid retrieval pipeline (keyword + vector + MMR), query strategies (semantic vs exact), `memory_search` → `memory_get` workflow, tuning parameters (`maxResults`, `minScore`)
4. **Memory Maintenance** — Heartbeat-based periodic review, cleanup strategies, `heartbeat-state.json` tracking
5. **Advanced Patterns** — Entity tracking with stable headings, opinion evolution with timestamps, cross-session recall via `sessionMemory`
6. **Troubleshooting** — No results, keyword-only matches, `memory_get` file restrictions, MEMORY.md bloat, missing transcript search

### Navigation

Added to `docs.json` under Sessions and memory → Memory group, right after `concepts/memory`.

### Source references

- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.ts` — default settings (maxResults: 6, maxSnippetChars: 700)
- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/read-file.ts` — `memory_get` file boundary enforcement
- `src/agents/memory-search.ts` — search config resolution and sources
- `docs/concepts/memory-builtin.md` — existing builtin engine docs

Closes #43032

target.title: [codex] prefer terminal reply fields in CLI JSONL parser
target.body:
﻿﻿## Summary

- Problem: the CLI JSONL parser only special-cases Claude `type:"result"` events and otherwise falls back to generic text collection, so OpenAI/Codex-style fallback runs can lose terminal reply semantics.
- Why it matters: in channel-visible fallback paths, commentary/intermediate text can be surfaced instead of the actual final answer even when the JSONL stream already contains `phase:"final_answer"` and `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
- What changed: `parseCliJsonl()` now prefers `task_complete.last_agent_message`, then assistant messages tagged `phase:"final_answer"`, and skips `commentary`-phase generic item text.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Claude CLI handling is unchanged, no dispatch/channel code changed, and no provider failover logic changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #59918
- Related #25592
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `parseCliJsonl()` was written around Claude `type:"result"` lines plus a generic `parsed.item.text` fallback, but it did not understand newer OpenAI/Codex-style JSONL terminal fields.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no parser preference for `task_complete.last_agent_message` or `phase:"final_answer"`, and no guard against treating `commentary` item text as the outbound reply.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current behavior is discussed in #59918 as a narrower parser-path issue adjacent to the broader channel-leak family in #25592.
- Why this regressed now: unknown as a specific regression point; this appears more like a compatibility gap exposed by fallback runs that emit richer JSONL event shapes.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: this fix does not depend on channel dispatching, failover selection, or Claude stream-json parsing.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when JSONL contains commentary, `final_answer`, and `task_complete`, the parser returns the terminal answer; when only `final_answer` is present, it wins over commentary text.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely inside the JSONL parsing function and can be reproduced with synthetic line fixtures without needing a live provider or channel.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing Claude result tests in `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` cover the Claude-specific path and remain unchanged.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenAI/Codex-style CLI fallback runs now prefer terminal reply fields instead of surfacing commentary when both are present in JSONL.
- Commentary-only generic item text is no longer treated as the final reply in this parser path.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
CLI JSONL -> generic text collection -> commentary can win -> user sees wrong reply

After:
CLI JSONL -> task_complete / final_answer preferred -> terminal answer wins -> user sees final reply
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (local contributor checkout)
- Runtime/container: Node.js + pnpm
- Model/provider: parser coverage for OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output; Claude path rechecked via existing tests
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A for unit verification
- Relevant config (redacted): CLI backend `output: "jsonl"`

### Steps

1. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary events plus a terminal `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
2. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary plus assistant `phase:"final_answer"` content.
3. Run the targeted parser tests.

### Expected

- Terminal answer fields win over commentary.
- Claude JSONL result handling still passes unchanged.

### Actual

- Verified with targeted tests after the patch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/cli-output.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked: existing Claude stream-json tests still pass; `task_complete` outranks `final_answer`; `final_answer` outranks commentary; commentary `item.text` is ignored.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not run a live WhatsApp/Telegram fallback end-to-end against a real CLI provider from this branch.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output is now parsed so terminal reply records win over commentary or intermediate message text in both the full-buffer parser and streaming parser.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout at PR head `8b90301ef3`; installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5`; Node `v24.13.1`; OpenClaw parser entry points imported directly from `src/agents/cli-output.ts`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK" 1> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.jsonl 2> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.stderr.txt

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports parseCliJsonl() and createCliJsonlStreamingParser() from ./src/agents/cli-output.ts
feeds the saved Codex CLI JSONL transcript through both parser paths

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports the same parser entry points and replays redacted Codex/OpenAI terminal-field JSONL containing commentary, final_answer, and task_complete records
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"
{"type":"thread.started","thread_id":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95"}
{"type":"turn.started"}
{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_0","type":"agent_message","text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"}}
{"type":"turn.completed","usage":{"input_tokens":22858,"cached_input_tokens":2432,"output_tokens":129,"reasoning_output_tokens":116}}

real_codex_full_buffer_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
real_codex_streaming_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
```

```text
terminal_field_jsonl=
{"type":"response_start","thread_id":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
{"item":{"type":"message","phase":"commentary","text":"COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN"}}
{"type":"response_item","payload":{"type":"message","role":"assistant","phase":"final_answer","content":[{"type":"output_text","text":"FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN"}]}}
{"type":"event_msg","payload":{"type":"task_complete","last_agent_message":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS"}}

{"text":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS","sessionId":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
streaming_output=
{"text":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS","sessionId":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
```

Observed result after fix: The installed Codex CLI produced JSONL on this machine and both OpenClaw parser paths extracted the final agent message from that transcript. For the bot-cited terminal-field shape, both parser paths returned `TASK_COMPLETE_WINS`; `COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN` and the lower-priority `FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN` were not surfaced.

What was not tested: A live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery route was not exercised for this parser-only PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a non-Claude JSONL backend could intentionally rely on commentary-phase `item.text` as the only final output.
  - Mitigation: the parser now only skips explicit `commentary` phase text and prefers explicit terminal fields; generic non-commentary `item.text` fallback remains intact.

target.title: fix(agents): handle explicit empty tool lists in system prompt
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets:

- Problem: when tools are explicitly disabled for a session, OpenClaw can still build a prompt that looks partly like a normal tool-enabled session. That can leave behind leaked skills content or other tool-only guidance.
- Why it matters: if the prompt says the session has tools when it actually does not, the model can try to use tools that are unavailable and behave like a tool-enabled session instead of a chat-only one.
- What changed: this PR makes the no-tools case consistent in the code that builds and adjusts the system prompt. The final prompt, prompt report, CLI path, embedded-runner path, and OpenAI-specific no-tools wording now all agree when a session really has no tools. It also fixes skills reporting in cases where the prompt contains another <available_skills> block or a skill body contains headings like # Project Context. Before this change, the prompt report could read the wrong block or stop too early, which could make it show missing or partial skills even though the real prompt still contained them.
- Additional behavior corrected: sessions that only have hosted/client tools no longer advertise OpenClaw built-in tool flows such as exec or subagents when those built-in tools are not actually available.
- Small contract update: provider-specific prompt code now receives `toolNames` in the `transformSystemPrompt` context, so plugins see the same advertised tool list that the main prompt builder is using.
- Scope boundary: this PR does not broadly redesign all capability handling. Sessions with omitted tool lists or non-empty filtered tool lists are still left mostly unchanged unless a narrow fix was needed to keep the no-tools and hosted-tool-only cases truthful.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related #25569, #53000, #46214
- Adjacent context: #44229, #44484, #47583
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: when OpenClaw builds the system prompt for a session with no tools, different parts of the prompt-building flow can disagree about whether tools are available. Some parts correctly treat the session as tool-free, while others still act as if tools or skills exist, so the final prompt can end up mixing no-tool behavior with tool-related guidance.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no coverage that locked in this behavior across the full flow, including the prompt text, prompt report, CLI path, embedded-runner path, provider-specific prompt code, and OpenAI-specific no-tools wording.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): #25569 is still the closest related upstream work because it distinguishes explicitly empty tool availability from unspecified/default availability, but other code paths still had inconsistent behavior. #53000 addresses similar empty-tools confusion but still keeps a reference tool catalog in the prompt. #46214 removes adjacent CLI-runner prompt pollution rather than this exact bug.
- Why this reproduces: the no-tools state was not carried through every step that contributes to the final prompt. Once a real runtime session had tools intentionally disabled, some later steps still added skills content or other tool-related guidance.
- What was ruled out: this is not just a unit-test-only mismatch. The same problem was reproduced through the normal OpenClaw CLI against an isolated deployment config, and review also surfaced related inconsistencies in provider-specific prompt code and OpenAI no-tools wording.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-report.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.memory.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-name-allowlist.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.test.ts`
  - `extensions/openai/index.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - explicit empty-tool sessions must not leak the skills catalog
  - explicit empty-tool sessions must not leak later tool-only guidance such as tool-call style, subagent/tool messaging guidance, approvals, or sandbox guidance
  - explicit empty-tool sessions must preserve truthful non-tool context such as workspace notes and TTS hints
  - prompt reports for explicit empty-tool sessions must keep `tools.entries = []` and no skills block
  - omitted `toolNames` must preserve legacy/default prompt behavior
  - explicit non-empty filtered tool lists must stay close to upstream/main behavior
  - client-tool-only sessions must not advertise native OpenClaw exec/subagent flows
  - provider overlays and transform hooks must receive the same effective tool context as the final prompt
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the final bug surface spans prompt construction, prompt reporting, runner plumbing, and provider overlays, so the reliable guardrail has to cover those seams rather than only the original builder helper.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none previously separated omitted tools from explicit empty effective tools across the whole prompt pipeline, and none previously locked in the client-tool-only/provider-transform cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Sessions with tools explicitly disabled now behave like true no-tool sessions:

- no leaked skills catalog
- no later tool-only guidance such as tool-call style, approval flow, subagent orchestration, tool-only messaging, or sandbox execution hints
- prompt report for the explicit no-tools path no longer includes a skills block
- OpenAI no-tools wording now stays aligned with the no-tools path instead of reintroducing tool-related wording
- workspace notes and TTS hints can still appear when supplied because they are context, not tool availability

Additional fixes covered by this branch:

- sessions that only have hosted/client tools no longer advertise OpenClaw built-in exec/subagent flows they do not actually have
- CLI and bundled-tool sessions continue to advertise truthful tool guidance when tools really are available
- provider-specific prompt code now receives the tool-name context it needs to make consistent prompt adjustments

Sessions with omitted tool lists and explicit non-empty filtered tool lists are still left close to existing behavior unless a narrow consistency fix required otherwise.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[toolNames omitted or []] -> [shared prompt construction] -> [explicit no-tools sessions can still inherit skills content or tool-related guidance]

After:
[toolNames omitted] -> [legacy/default prompt behavior]
[toolNames non-empty] -> [upstream/main-style filtered prompt behavior]
[toolNames = []] -> [explicit no-tools branch] -> [truthful no-tool prompt with no leaked skills block or tool-only guidance]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Debian bookworm host)
- Runtime/container: local source checkout run via pnpm; not a containerized repro
- Model/provider: `openai-codex/gpt-5.4`
- Integration/channel (if any): CLI via `openclaw agent --local`
- Relevant config (redacted): temporary `no-tools` agent with `tools: { deny: ["*"] }`

### Steps

1. Create a disposable config copy and add a temporary no-tool agent so the runtime has a truly empty effective tool set:

   ```bash
   export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/path/to/openclaw.json
   export OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/path/to/state-dir

   cp "$OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH" /tmp/openclaw-no-tools-repro.json
   export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-no-tools-repro.json

   python3 - <<'PY'
   import json, os
   p = os.environ["OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH"]
   with open(p) as f:
       cfg = json.load(f)
   agents = cfg.setdefault("agents", {}).setdefault("list", [])
   agents = [a for a in agents if a.get("id") != "no-tools"]
   agents.append({
       "id": "no-tools",
       "tools": {"deny": ["*"]},
   })
   cfg["agents"]["list"] = agents
   with open(p, "w") as f:
       json.dump(cfg, f, indent=2)
       f.write("\n")
   PY
   ```

2. Run the normal CLI repro against that config:

   ```bash
   openclaw agent --local \
     --agent no-tools \
     --session-id explicit-empty-tools-repro \
     --json \
     --message '/context detail' \
     --timeout 120 \
   | jq '{payloads, tools: .meta.systemPromptReport.tools, skills: .meta.systemPromptReport.skills, systemPrompt: .meta.systemPromptReport.systemPrompt}'
   ```

### Expected

- In a real explicit no-tool session:
  - `tools.entries` should be `[]`
  - `tools.listChars` should be `0`
  - the prompt should not inject the skills catalog
  - the prompt should explicitly behave like a no-tools session

### Actual

- Live deployed-instance repro on this fixed branch:
  - `tools.entries = []`
  - `tools.listChars = 0`
  - `skills.promptChars = 0`
  - `skills.entries = []`
  - `systemPrompt.chars = 20194`
  - final assistant text includes `No tools are available in this session.`
- Live deployed-instance repro on v2026.04.11:
  - `tools.entries = []`
  - `tools.listChars = 0`
  - `skills.promptChars = 5932`
  - `skills.entries.length = 11`
  - `systemPrompt.chars = 30389`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Evidence highlights:
- Live repro on this branch:
  - `tools.entries = []`
  - `tools.listChars = 0`
  - `skills.promptChars = 0`
  - `skills.entries = []`
  - `systemPrompt.chars = 20194`
  - assistant text explicitly says `No tools are available in this session.`
- Live repro on `v2026.4.11`:
  - `tools.entries = []`
  - `tools.listChars = 0`
  - `skills.promptChars = 5932`
  - `skills.entries.length = 11`
  - `systemPrompt.chars = 30389`
- Targeted prompt/report tests on this branch:
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-report.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.memory.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-name-allowlist.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.test.ts`
  - `extensions/openai/index.test.ts`
  - result: `150` tests passed across `8` files

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - reran the deployed-instance repro on an isolated OpenClaw instance using a disposable no-tools config
  - confirmed the bug still reproduces on `main` and `v2026.4.11`
  - confirmed the same repro no longer fails on this fixed branch
  - confirmed the verification signal is prompt/report metadata, not model-specific assistant output
- Edge cases checked:
  - explicit empty-tool session keeps `tools.entries = []` and `tools.listChars = 0`
  - explicit empty-tool session no longer leaks the skills catalog or later tool-only guidance
  - client-tool-only sessions no longer advertise native OpenClaw exec/subagent flows they do not actually have
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full OpenClaw test suite
  - unrelated model-level or workflow-level regressions outside this prompt-construction/reporting bug
  - performance impact beyond the prompt/report character-count change above

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: callers that accidentally relied on tool-related guidance surviving in explicit empty-tool sessions will now get a true no-tool prompt.
  - Mitigation: that behavior now matches explicit runtime intent, and regression tests lock in the distinction.
- Risk: future prompt edits could blur the difference between omitted, explicit empty, and explicit non-empty filtered tool states.
  - Mitigation: regression tests now cover all three cases.

target.title: feat(doctor): add Node.js runtime info health contribution
target.body:
## Summary
Add a new Doctor health contribution that displays Node.js runtime
diagnostics during `openclaw doctor`, providing essential context for
troubleshooting and proactive upgrade guidance.
## What it does
**Runtime summary note** (always shown):
Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
**Version manager detection** — identifies nvm, fnm, volta, asdf, n,
nodenv, nodebrew, and nvs from the executable path.
**Proactive warnings** (when applicable):
- Node version below minimum (>=22.19.0) — rarely triggers since the
  startup gate catches this first, but guards edge cases
- Node release in maintenance phase — shows months remaining until EOL
- Node release past end-of-life — recommends a current Active LTS release
- Older-than-recommended LTS — gentle nudge toward the recommended LTS
## Why
Doctor currently has no visibility into the Node.js runtime environment.
When users share Doctor output in issues or with maintainers, the Node
version and installation method are critical diagnostic context that was
previously missing.
This reuses existing infrastructure:
- `runtime-guard.ts` — `detectRuntime()`, `parseSemver()`, `runtimeSatisfies()`
- `runtime-paths.ts` — `isVersionManagedNodePath()`
No new dependencies. No runtime behavior changes outside of Doctor.
## Changes
New files:
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.ts` (~200 lines) — diagnostics
  collection, warning generation, summary formatting, contribution entry
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.test.ts` — 27 unit tests
Modified:
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts` — +1 import, +1 wrapper
  function, +1 contribution registration (after `bundled-plugin-runtime-deps`,
  before `state-integrity`)
## Test plan
- `pnpm check` — 0 errors ✅
- `vitest` — 27/27 tests ✅
- `pnpm build` — clean ✅
Test coverage:
- `detectVersionManagerName`: 11 tests (null, system, nvm, fnm, volta,
  asdf, n, nodenv, nodebrew, nvs, Windows backslash)
- `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics`: 3 tests (nvm path, unknown runtime,
  system install)
- `buildNodeRuntimeWarnings`: 8 tests (recommended version, below
  minimum, past EOL, maintenance phase, pre-maintenance nudge, current
  recommended, null major, future major)
- `buildNodeRuntimeSummary`: 5 tests (system install, named manager,
  unknown manager, null version, separator format)
---
## Real behavior proof
_Refreshed for current head `f90eee0a` (after the Windows case-insensitive OS-home redaction commit)._

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The Doctor Node runtime note renders the executable path via a two-pass redaction (`shortenHomePath` for the OpenClaw home, then an OS-home pass). The OS-home pass compared the Windows home prefix with exact casing, so a valid Windows path whose drive/username casing differed from `os.homedir()` (e.g. `os.homedir()` = `C:\Users\Alice`, `execPath` = `c:\users\alice\...`) fell through unredacted and leaked the OS username. POSIX comparison stays case-sensitive; the Windows backslash-boundary prefix is now compared case-insensitively, slicing by the original length to preserve real casing.

- **Real environment tested:** Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head `f90eee0a`. Invoked the real `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics()` + `buildNodeRuntimeSummary()` — the same render `noteNodeRuntime()` emits in the Doctor health flow — plus injected lifecycle scenarios via the exported helpers.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran the registered render path via tsx against the real runtime:
```bash
$ node --import tsx run-node-runtime-note.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix (current head):** Block 1 is the real render on this machine. Block 2 is the POSIX OS-home redaction scenario (OPENCLAW_HOME elsewhere). Block 3 is the new Windows case-insensitive redaction this commit fixes. Remaining blocks are injected lifecycle scenarios against the current date.
```
=== 1. Real current-head render (Node v24.14.0) ===
Node 24.14.0 · /usr/bin/node · system install

=== 2. POSIX OS-home redaction (OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw, Node under the user's OS home) ===
os home (HOME)     : /home/<user>
input  execPath    : /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== 3. Windows case-insensitive redaction (the new fix) ===
os.homedir()       : C:\Users\Alice   (canonical casing)
input  execPath    : c:\users\alice\AppData\Roaming\nvm\v24.14.0\node.exe   (lower-cased)
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~\AppData\Roaming\nvm\v24.14.0\node.exe · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== Injected Node 22 LTS (22.19.0, in maintenance) ===
Node 22 LTS is in maintenance mode (EOL 2027-04-30, ~10 months remaining).
Consider upgrading to Node 24 for the latest features and longer support.

=== Injected Node 20 (20.18.0, below the >=22.19.0 minimum) ===
Node 20.18.0 does not meet the minimum requirement (>=22.19.0).
Upgrade Node: https://nodejs.org/en/download
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real Node 24.14.0 runtime renders a clean note (supported, system install). Both the POSIX OS-home scenario (block 2) and the Windows case-mismatch scenario (block 3) now redact the home to `~`, so no OS username appears — including when `execPath` casing differs from `os.homedir()`. Before this commit, the exact-case Windows prefix check left such paths unredacted. Injected Node 22 LTS / 20 produce the expected maintenance / below-minimum advisories. POSIX, Windows OPENCLAW_HOME-override, and the new Windows case-mismatch regression tests cover the redaction paths; 51 unit tests pass, types and lint clean.

- **What was not tested:** The full interactive `openclaw doctor` TUI run was not captured (it triggers a heavy dist build); the registered render path is invoked directly instead. Redaction relies on `os.homedir()`; in the rare case where `HOME`/`USERPROFILE` is unset and differs from the path's owner (e.g. some sudo/container setups), redaction may not apply — out of scope for this detect-only diagnostic.

target.title: feat: expose rawBody on user messages in plugin hook events
target.body:
### Summary

Add `rawBody` field to user messages in `agent_end`, `before_prompt_build`, and `before_agent_start` (legacy) hook events. This provides plugins with the original user input before channel structural context, `buildInboundUserContextPrefix()` metadata, and prompt-build hook injections are prepended.

### Motivation

Plugin hooks receive `event.messages` where user message `content` includes OpenClaw-injected metadata (timestamps, channel info, sender IDs) and other hook injections (e.g., memory context). Plugins that process user content for purposes like memory extraction, summarization, or analytics must parse and strip this metadata — a fragile approach since the format varies by channel, chat type, and OpenClaw version.

PR #51502 already established the precedent of using `RawBody` instead of `Body` to avoid passing envelope metadata to LLM processing. This PR extends that principle to plugin hook events.

### Changes

- Compute `rawBodyForHooks` from `CommandBody ?? RawBody ?? Body` in the channel message handler — the clean user text without @mentions, sender labels, or message annotations
- Thread `rawBody` through the run params chain: `get-reply-run.ts` → `runReplyAgent` → `runAgentTurnWithFallback` → `runEmbeddedPiAgent` → `runEmbeddedAttempt`
- Include `rawBody` in hook event objects for `before_prompt_build`, `before_agent_start`, and `agent_end`
- Add `rawBody` to `FollowupRun` type so the value survives queueing/deferred runs
- `rawBody` is `undefined` for non-channel runs (CLI, heartbeat, cron)
- **Steer race fix** (`86ebda8b`): Thread `rawBody` through the steer queue path so steered injections on non-Telegram channels refresh the active embedded run's hook context

### Backward Compatibility

- Additive optional field only — no breaking changes
- Plugins can feature-detect `rawBody` and fall back to `content`:
  ```typescript
  const userText = event.rawBody ?? extractText(lastUser.content);
  ```
- Plugin SDK API baseline unchanged (no drift)

### Use Case

Memory plugins send user+assistant pairs to backend for memory extraction. Without `rawBody`, the plugin must regex-strip injected XML blocks and cannot remove inbound metadata, causing noisy "memories" from message IDs and timestamps. With `rawBody`, the plugin gets clean user input directly.

### Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Plugin hook events (`before_prompt_build`, `agent_end`) now expose `event.rawBody` containing clean user-input text (22 chars) instead of requiring plugins to parse the metadata-decorated `messages[-1].content` (411 chars). Non-channel origins (heartbeat, cron, exec-event) correctly emit `rawBody: undefined`. Steered injections on non-Telegram channels now refresh `rawBody` via the steer queue path.

Real environment tested: Linux 6.17.0-1013-aws (arm64), Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.20, Bedrock `global.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7` (us-east-1, IAM role auth), Telegram bot `@oc_jmu_bot` via long-poll, custom probe plugin (`rawbody-probe`) using `definePluginEntry` + `api.on("before_prompt_build")` + `api.on("agent_end")`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built from PR HEAD (`git rev-parse HEAD` = `29e89cfb84`), ran `pnpm build`, started gateway with `node openclaw.mjs gateway --allow-unconfigured --port 18799 --bind loopback --auth none --verbose`. Sent Telegram DM "hello rawbody test 004" to `@oc_jmu_bot`. Triggered heartbeat via `node openclaw.mjs system event --text "..." --mode now --url ws://127.0.0.1:18799 --token anonymous --json`. Ran unit tests: `pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts -t "rawBody hook gating" --reporter=verbose`.

Evidence after fix:
```
Case A — Channel happy path (Telegram DM):
[rawbody-probe] runId    = 4c1c5a9b-8e2c-405f-b6f3-d2246e4912cc
[rawbody-probe] trigger  = user
[rawbody-probe] rawBody  = "hello rawbody test 004"   (22 chars)
[rawbody-probe] lastUser = "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\n...
                            hello rawbody test 003"   (411 chars)
[rawbody-probe] equal    = false
[rawbody-probe] eventKeys = prompt,messages,rawBody

Case B — Heartbeat origin:
[rawbody-probe] runId    = 40d31407-6882-4f23-bdc7-85ba18fb5a41
[rawbody-probe] trigger  = heartbeat
[rawbody-probe] rawBody  = <undefined>
[rawbody-probe] lastUser = "[OpenClaw heartbeat poll]"
[rawbody-probe] eventKeys = messages,success,error,durationMs,rawBody,runId

Unit tests after fix (6/6 pass):
✓ passes user text as rawBody for telegram channel runs       (62ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for heartbeat runs               (2ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for cron-event runs              (1ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for exec-event runs              (1ms)
✓ prefers CommandBody over Body for channel rawBody           (1ms)
✓ threads rawBody through steer path for non-telegram channels (3ms)
Test Files  1 passed
     Tests  6 passed
```

Observed result after fix: `event.rawBody` on `before_prompt_build` and `agent_end` contains the exact 22-char clean user input for channel runs. Non-channel origins correctly emit `undefined`. The steer path unit test confirms `followupRun.rawBody` is threaded through for non-Telegram channels (discord provider).

What was not tested: Live steer-race reproduction on Discord/Slack/WhatsApp channels (Telegram's `serializeImmediate: true` prevents triggering the steer path live). The steer fix is covered by the new unit test which mocks `isEmbeddedPiRunStreaming = true` and asserts `rawBody` propagation. No multi-user concurrency test was performed.

target.title: docs(gateway): document high-memory local Ollama compaction tuning
target.body:
﻿Fixes #55888

## What Problem This Solves

High-memory local Ollama users have existing compaction knobs that can keep more recent transcript in large-context sessions, but the local models guide did not explain the supported profile, threshold math, or safe verification path. Users had to infer why a 32k context model started memory flush or preflight compaction earlier than expected.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds a focused docs-only profile to the local models guide for 32 GB / 64 GB+ Ollama setups. It documents the existing `agents.defaults.compaction` keys, explains the `contextWindow - reserveTokensFloor - memoryFlush.softThresholdTokens` threshold calculation, and keeps runtime defaults unchanged for lower-memory machines.

## User Impact

Users running larger local Ollama contexts can now tune compaction intentionally, see the expected 32,768 - 2,000 - 4,000 = 26,768 threshold example, and confirm the effective `contextWindow` and `threshold` in verbose Gateway logs before relying on a long session.

## Evidence

AI-assisted: yes. I used Codex to inspect the issue, source-backed threshold behavior, docs rules, and validation output. I reviewed the final diff and understand the change.

Validation run locally:

```text
git diff --check
```
