target.title: External reranker
target.body:
## Summary What problem does this PR solve? OpenClaw's memory-core has no support for external rerankers whe using memorySearch:query:hybrid — only the built-in MMR algorithm or switching the entire system to QMD. QMD is local-only, so the reranker runs on the gateway. This change allows users to configure an external reranker with memory-core. Why does this matter now? It increases the quality of results from memory searches. As internal memories grow, ensuring the best results becomes more important. What is the intended outcome? Better results from memory searches. What is intentionally out of scope? QMD, LanceDB, and other memory systems for Openclaw. What does success look like? Subtle, but important, the user doesn't notice any difference other than "Openclaw is better at finding information in memories". What should reviewers focus on? Look at the interface used between memory-core and MMR. MMR has been moved to a plugin, and the new external reranker uses the same interface.
Summary guidance This PR makes two changes. First, it moves mmr to a bundled plugin called memory-mmr. Second, it adds a memory-external-reranker plugin to memory-core, enabling external reranker support for Cohere-compatible /v1/rerank endpoints. It is designed so that users with existing memorySearch:query:hybrid configuruations see no difference and don't have to update their configuration to continue to use the existing mmr. Only when the user enables and memory-external-reranker plugin and adds the other necessary config entries will the external reranker be used. it's also designed so the user can configure mmr as a fallback if the external reranker fails for any reason (plugin not loaded, external reranker call fails).
## Linked context Which issue does this close? Closes #64438 Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? Related # Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
Linked context guidance https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64438
## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: external reranker called during memory recall - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Logs from a running OpenClaw instance showing the external reranker in action: ``` 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] reranking with provider=local-llm-server-02-8082 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 fallbacks=none topN=30 documents=34 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 url=http://local-llm-server-02:8082/v1/rerank topN=30 documents=34 ``` ... ``` 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 response status=200 ok=true 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 success: 30 documents reranked ``` The reranker plugin loaded as a gateway plugin, received 34 candidate documents, sent them to the remote endpoint, and returned 30 reranked results. - Observed result after fix: better search results from memory - What was not tested: QMD, LanceDB, other memory systems - Proof limitations or environment constraints: - Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
Real behavior proof guidance config json fragments agents:defaults: ``` "memorySearch": { "enabled": true, "provider": "openai-compatible", "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "query": { "hybrid": { "enabled": true, "vectorWeight": 0.7, "textWeight": 0.3, "mmr": { "enabled": true, "lambda": 0.7, "provider": "memory-external-reranker", "fallback": "memory-mmr" } } }, "remote": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8081/v1", "apiKey": { "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "LOCAL_LLM_02_KEY" }, "batch": { "enabled": false }, "nonBatchConcurrency": 3 }, } ``` models:providers: ``` "local-llm-server-02-8082": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8082", "models": [ { "id": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "name": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "input": [ "text" ] } ] } ``` plugins:entries: ``` "memory-external-reranker": { "enabled": true, "config": { "provider": "local-llm-server-02-8082", "model": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "topN": 30 } }, ``` - Real environment tested: multiple test VMs. I've been running it on my production Openclaw instance for over a week. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enable and configure the memory-external-reranker plugin, index memory, perform a memory search like "What do you remember about the Lucid Air?" See attached screenshot.
## Tests and validation Which commands did you run? pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test pnpm test:extension memory-external-reranker pnpm test:extension memory-mmr What regression coverage was added or updated? Full tests added for both MMR plugin and new external reranker plugin. What failed before this fix, if known? If no test was added, why not?
Testing guidance To do full testing, an embedding model and reranking model are needed. I use llamacpp on localhost and update openclaw.json to point to the local models. ``` ./llama-server -hf Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF --port 8081 --embedding ./llama-server -hf ggml-org/Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B-Q8_0-GGUF --port 8082 --embedding --reranking --pooling rank ```
## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? No Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Yes. New config entries added Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No. Uses existing patterns and code to call external reranker. What is the highest-risk area? Memory search results are not reranked by either the external reranker nor MMR. How is that risk mitigated? MMR is a bundled plugin. As long as mmr is a buldled plugin and loaded by default, the risk is minimal.
Risk guidance Minimal risk once MMR is a bundled plugin.
## Current review state What is the next action? What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
Review state guidance Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

target.title: fix(sanitize): preserve user-visible prose after error-prefix lines
target.body:
## Summary

When an assistant message stopped with `stopReason="error"` and the visible text started with an error prefix (e.g. `"Error:"`, `"request failed:"`), `sanitizeUserFacingText` replaced the whole payload with a templated error label. Any user-visible prose that followed the error line — recommendations, next steps, commands to try — was silently dropped from the channel reply.

- **Problem**: `sanitizeUserFacingText` error-context path rewrites the entire text when `ERROR_PREFIX_RE` matches, discarding multi-line prose that follows the error prefix
- **Solution**: Classify the leading error line through existing safe formatters, then append only safe follow-up prose — skipping raw stack frames, JSON payload bodies, and HTML error pages
- **What changed**: `sanitize-user-facing-text.ts` (+76 lines: `isUnsafeMultilineErrorBody` guard, `classifyErrorPrefixLine` helper, multi-line suffix detection in `errorContext` branch) + `sanitize-user-facing-text.test.ts` (new, 148 lines, 11 tests)
- **What did NOT change**: Single-line error fragments still flow into the existing rewrite logic unchanged; `errorContext: false` path is unaffected; no changes to callers or upstream text assembly

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #96007
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

When a Discord bot sends a multi-part reply and one section contains an inline tool error, users only see the error line — all subsequent recommendations and next steps are silently dropped. This makes it impossible for users to see the full agent response. The root cause is in `sanitizeUserFacingText`, which is channel-agnostic and affects all channel types, not just Discord.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: Assistant reply text containing an error-prefix line followed by multi-line user-visible prose was replaced entirely with a templated error label, losing recommendations/next steps. Additionally, raw stack frames and JSON error payloads could leak into channel replies.

- **Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node 24.13.1, branch `fix/sanitize-error-context-96007`, commit `5770b27`

- **Evidence after fix — inline probe**:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e '
import { sanitizeUserFacingText } from "./src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/sanitize-user-facing-text.js";

// 1. timeout + recommendations (multi-line prose preserved)
const r1 = sanitizeUserFacingText(
  "Error: connection timed out\n\nRecommendation: try restarting the service.\nNext steps:\n1. Check logs\n2. Retry",
  { errorContext: true }
);
console.log("Test 1 - Has Recommendation:", r1.includes("Recommendation"));
console.log("Test 1 - Has timed out:", r1.includes("timed out"));

// 2. transport error + prose
const r2 = sanitizeUserFacingText(
  "Error: fetch failed. SocketError: other side closed\n\nHere is a summary of findings:\n  Item A is healthy\n  Item B is healthy\n\nRecommendation: try restarting.",
  { errorContext: true }
);
console.log("Test 2 - Has summary:", r2.includes("summary of findings"));
console.log("Test 2 - Has LLM request failed:", r2.includes("LLM request failed"));
'
Test 1 - Has Recommendation: true
Test 1 - Has timed out: true
Test 2 - Has summary: true
Test 2 - Has LLM request failed: true
```

- **Evidence after fix — stack/JSON/HTML guards**:

```console
// 3. Stack trace suffix — MUST NOT leak
$ node --import tsx -e '
const r = sanitizeUserFacingText(
  "Error: ECONNREFUSED\n    at Server.onError (server.ts:42:8)\n    at emitError (events.js:153:12)\n    at processTicksAndRejections (internal/process/task_queues.js:95:5)",
  { errorContext: true }
);
console.log("server.ts leaked:", r.includes("server.ts"));
console.log("events.js leaked:", r.includes("events.js"));
console.log("Has connection refused:", r.includes("connection refused"));
'
server.ts leaked: false
events.js leaked: false
Has connection refused: true

// 4. JSON payload body — MUST NOT leak
$ node --import tsx -e '
const r = sanitizeUserFacingText(
  "Error: fetch failed\n{\"error\":{\"type\":\"server_error\",\"message\":\"internal\"}}\n{\"request_id\":\"req_abc123\"}",
  { errorContext: true }
);
console.log("server_error leaked:", r.includes("server_error"));
console.log("req_abc123 leaked:", r.includes("req_abc123"));
'
server_error leaked: false
req_abc123 leaked: false

// 5. HTML error page — MUST NOT leak
$ node --import tsx -e '
const r = sanitizeUserFacingText(
  "Error: gateway error\n<!doctype html>\n<html><body>502 Bad Gateway</body></html>",
  { errorContext: true }
);
console.log("DOCTYPE leaked:", r.includes("<!doctype html>"));
console.log("502 leaked:", r.includes("502 Bad Gateway"));
'
DOCTYPE leaked: false
502 leaked: false

// 6. Single-line — still rewritten
$ node --import tsx -e 'console.log(sanitizeUserFacingText("Error: connection timed out", { errorContext: true }));'
LLM request timed out.

// 7. errorContext: false — passthrough
$ node --import tsx -e 'console.log(sanitizeUserFacingText("Error: timeout\n\nReco: retry", { errorContext: false }));'
Error: timeout

Reco: retry
```

- **Evidence after fix — full test suite**:

```console
$ pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/sanitize-user-facing-text.test.ts --reporter=verbose

 ✓ classifies timeout error line and appends recommendations
 ✓ classifies transport error line and appends safe multi-paragraph content
 ✓ classifies leading line and appends guidance after an API Error prefix
 ✓ returns only the classified error when suffix is a stack trace
 ✓ returns only the classified error when suffix is a JSON payload body
 ✓ returns only the classified error when suffix is an HTML error page
 ✓ appends safe prose after a classified leading line even when one line looks suspicious
 ✓ rewrites a bare Error: timeout line
 ✓ rewrites a bare transport error line (econnrefused)
 ✓ rewrites a transport error line without suffix content
 ✓ passes through full text when errorContext is false

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
   Start at  11:50:08
   Duration  527ms
```

**Before/after matrix**:

| Input | `errorContext` | Before fix | After fix |
|-------|---------------|-----------|----------|
| `Error: timeout\n\nReco: retry` | `true` | `LLM request timed out.` | classified error + prose preserved |
| `Error: Connection refused\n\nTry again` | `true` | `LLM request failed: connection refused...` | classified error + prose preserved |
| `request failed: conn reset\n\nSteps: 1. Check` | `true` | `LLM request failed: network connection was interrupted.` | classified error + prose preserved |
| `Error: ECONNREFUSED\n    at Server.onError (server.ts:42:8)\n    at emitError (...)` | `true` | `LLM request failed: connection refused...` | classified error, stack frames stripped |
| `Error: fetch failed\n{"error":{...}}` | `true` | `LLM request failed: network connection error.` | classified error, JSON body stripped |
| `Error: gateway error\n<!doctype html>...` | `true` | generic error label | classified error, HTML body stripped |
| `Error: timeout` (single line) | `true` | `LLM request timed out.` | `LLM request timed out.` (unchanged) |
| `Error: ECONNREFUSED` (single line) | `true` | `LLM request failed: connection refused...` | unchanged |
| Any text | `false` | Full text preserved | unchanged |

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. Multi-line prose after a classified error-prefix line is preserved in channel replies
  2. Stack traces (2+ stack-frame lines), JSON error payload bodies, and HTML error pages are stripped
  3. Single-line error fragments still produce the existing templated error labels
  4. Non-error context path (`errorContext: false`) is completely unaffected
  5. All 11 regression tests pass covering: prose preservation, stack/JSON/HTML stripping, single-line rewrite, and errorContext passthrough

- **What was not tested**: Live Discord roundtrip (requires a real Discord bot token and account); behavior on non-Linux platforms

## Root Cause

In `sanitizeUserFacingText`, when `errorContext` is `true` and the trimmed text matches `ERROR_PREFIX_RE`, the function unconditionally rewrote the entire payload into a short templated error label. It did not check whether the text contains substantive multi-line prose after the error prefix that should be preserved, nor did it guard against raw stack frames and error payload bodies leaking through.

The `errorContext: true` path is activated by `finalizeAssistantExtraction` in `embedded-agent-utils.ts:47-49` whenever `msg.stopReason === "error"`.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Channel replies that contain an error-prefix line followed by user-visible prose (e.g. recommendations, next steps) will now show the prose content after the error prefix, rather than being replaced entirely by a short error label. Raw stack frames, JSON error bodies, and HTML error pages are stripped from the suffix.

## Security Impact (required)

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No
- [x] Stack trace / internal path leakage addressed? Yes — `isUnsafeMultilineErrorBody` strips 2+ stack-frame suffixes and JSON/HTML bodies before appending prose

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: Multi-line error text preservation, stack trace stripping, JSON body stripping, HTML body stripping, single-line error rewrite, non-error-context passthrough
- **Edge cases checked**: Empty error prefix, empty suffix, single suspicious line (not enough to trigger stack detection), text with only the error prefix and no content after
- **What you did NOT verify**: Live Discord delivery, multi-channel behavior (Telegram/Slack/WhatsApp), interaction with streaming/progress drafts

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes
- [x] Config/env changes? No
- [x] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes. The guard is placed at the exact rewrite point inside `sanitizeUserFacingText`, which is the single funnel for all user-facing error text across all channels.
- **Refactor needed**: No. The existing error-rewrite logic is well-structured; the added helpers (`isUnsafeMultilineErrorBody`, `classifyErrorPrefixLine`) are narrow and self-contained.
- **Alternative considered**: Adding channel-specific guards in Discord's outbound adapter. Rejected because this is a text-sanitization problem, not a delivery problem — the fix benefits all channels uniformly.

## AI Assistance

target.title: fix(proxy): apply enhanced NO_PROXY matching to global undici dispatcher
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When `HTTPS_PROXY` is set in the environment, `ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher()` installs a global undici `EnvHttpProxyAgent` that routes ALL HTTP requests through the proxy — including internal plugin requests that should bypass it via NO_PROXY.

The built-in undici `EnvHttpProxyAgent` NO_PROXY matcher does not support:
- **Leading-dot subdomain patterns** like `.myqcloud.com`
- **CIDR notation** like `100.64.0.0/10`
- **Octet wildcards** like `100.64.*`

OpenClaw already has an enhanced `matchesNoProxy()` function, but it was only used by provider HTTP helpers — NOT by the global dispatcher.

### Fix (revised per ClawSweeper review)

1. **NO_PROXY-aware dispatcher wrapper**: Wrap the global EnvHttpProxyAgent so each dispatch checks OpenClaw's enhanced `matchesNoProxy()` before routing through the proxy.

2. **ALL_PROXY kept SSRF-safe**: Unlike the previous approach, `hasEnvHttpProxyConfigured()` (the SSRF-safe shared helper) stays HTTP(S)-only. The global dispatcher uses `hasEnvHttpProxyAgentConfigured()` directly, which correctly includes ALL_PROXY via `resolveEnvHttpProxyAgentOptions()`. This is safe because the global dispatcher already runs inside the EnvHttpProxyAgent.

3. **All construction paths wrapped**: `createNoProxyAwareEnvDispatcher()` is now applied in all three places that build an EnvHttpProxyAgent: bootstrap (`ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher`), timeout hardening (`applyGlobalDispatcherStreamTimeouts`), and proxy lifecycle reset (`forceResetGlobalDispatcher`).

## Evidence

### Test matrix

| Test file | Tests | Result |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| `proxy-env.test.ts` | 55 | ✅ Passed |
| `undici-global-dispatcher.test.ts` | 33 | ✅ Passed |
| **Total** | **88** | **✅ All passed** |

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Global undici dispatcher respects enhanced NO_PROXY matching (subdomain/CIDR/wildcard). ALL_PROXY deployments use the correct proxy routing without widening SSRF guards. Proxy lifecycle resets preserve the enhanced bypass.

- **Real environment tested**: Local source checkout, Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x64

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests  88 passed (88)
  proxy-env — 55 tests (ALL_PROXY still ignored by SSRF guard ✅)
  undici-global-dispatcher — 33 tests (reset path wrapped ✅)
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: ALL_PROXY-only environments route through the proxy. SSRF-guarded fetch paths stay HTTP(S)-only. All EnvHttpProxyAgent construction paths carry the enhanced wrapper. NO_PROXY bypass with subdomain/CIDR/wildcard works.

- **What was not tested**: End-to-end with a real proxy and qqbot COS upload (requires live credentials). The enhanced matching is covered by unit tests.

### Checks
- Format: ✅
- Diff: +15 / -10 (25 lines, <=300)
- Branch: clean from origin/main
- Lockfile: unchanged
- SSRF boundary: preserved (HTTP(S)-only)

target.title: feat(agents): add per-agent env subprocess contract
target.body:
Closes #93425

## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a problem where `agents.list[]` can define agent-specific behavior but had no supported config contract for passing safe per-agent environment variables into the subprocesses that actually run that agent's CLI, exec, ACP, ACPX, or stdio MCP work.

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Operators need a single `agents.list[].env` entry to affect the real spawned process boundaries for that agent, not just an in-memory agent config object.
- **Root cause:** The OpenClaw agent config schema and resolver did not expose an `env` contract, so CLI runner, exec tools, ACP session creation/reuse, ACPX session options, and stdio MCP transport creation had no shared source-of-truth for per-agent environment overrides.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Per-agent tools and runtimes can require stable environment variables for SDKs, credentials selectors, feature toggles, or integration probes; without a canonical config path, each subprocess boundary behaved differently.

## Why This Change Was Made

What is the intended outcome?

Root-cause fix: add `agents.list[].env` as the canonical string-map config contract and route its sanitized value from `resolveAgentConfig` into each real subprocess boundary that executes agent work.

What should reviewers focus on?

Review the source-of-truth path from `agents.list[].env` through `resolveAgentConfig`, then into CLI runner env assembly, exec env assembly, ACP initialize/re-ensure inputs, ACPX `SessionAgentOptions.env`, stdio MCP transport env, and `tools.effective` MCP summary fingerprinting.

What is intentionally out of scope?

No default environment variables were added, no migration is needed for existing configs, no server-specific MCP `env` precedence changed, no host env safety policy was relaxed, and no unrelated provider/auth/session behavior is changed.

- **Fix classification:** Root-cause fix for a missing public config/schema/protocol contract and its downstream subprocess propagation paths.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The patch fixes the contract source and every consumer boundary that spawns or reuses agent subprocess work; it does not add a one-off fallback at a single runner. MCP catalog fingerprints also include the same session-derived env so `tools.effective` projects the warm runtime instead of masking an env-scoped catalog as stale.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** `resolveAgentConfig(cfg, agentId)` remains the source-of-truth owner for per-agent options. `agents.list[].env` is copied out of that resolver and sanitized at process boundaries with the existing host-env security policy.
- **Patch quality notes:** Patch quality warnings are expected from small guard returns in ACPX model-capability error detection and test-only config casts. These are not fallback masking; they keep behavior explicit while the env contract remains owned by config schema/resolver and subprocess boundary sanitization.
- **Related open PR / canonical PR scan:** ACPX #389 is already merged and exposes `SessionAgentOptions.env`; this PR consumes that upstream contract in OpenClaw rather than competing with it. The downstream scope is OpenClaw #93425.
- **What did NOT change:** Out of scope: existing configs without `agents.list[].env` behave the same; dangerous `NODE_OPTIONS`, loader/preload, shell-hook, and PATH-style overrides remain ignored; MCP server-local `env` still wins over inherited agent env; request/plugin/channel exec env precedence remains unchanged.
- **Risk labels considered:** public config/schema/protocol, compatibility, session-state, security-boundary, availability.
- **Risk explanation:** The highest merge risk is security-boundary and compatibility risk because env propagation touches child processes and session/tool runtime reuse. The change is opt-in, string-only, and uses OpenClaw's existing sanitizer instead of introducing a new allow/deny policy.
- **Why acceptable:** Safe env keys are forwarded only where the selected agent's work is already spawned; dangerous startup overrides are filtered before child processes; tests cover direct subprocess, exec, ACP, ACPX, stdio MCP, and `tools.effective` warm-catalog behavior.

## User Impact

Operators can configure safe per-agent environment variables once on the agent entry and have them show up consistently when that agent starts CLI subprocesses, runs exec tools, initializes/restarts ACP sessions, starts ACPX-backed sessions, or launches stdio MCP servers.

Dangerous startup overrides such as `NODE_OPTIONS` and `PATH` are not forwarded through this new contract.

## Evidence

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.resolve-env-hook.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
- Local child-process proof script, with copied output below
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-post-main-sync.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src-post-main-sync.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Config schema accepts `agents.list[].env` string maps and rejects non-string values.
- Agent resolver returns env only for the selected agent.
- CLI runner and exec subprocess env include safe agent env and drop blocked startup overrides.
- ACP initialize and runtime re-ensure paths pass sanitized env into runtime session inputs.
- ACPX runtime maps OpenClaw env into `SessionAgentOptions.env`.
- Stdio MCP transport inherits sanitized agent env while preserving server-local env precedence.
- Session MCP runtime fingerprints include sanitized inherited env.
- `tools.effective` passes `sessionKey` into the MCP summary and includes `sessionKey`/`agentId` in its summary cache key, so warm env-scoped MCP catalogs are not misreported as stale.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Before the fix, there was no schema/resolver contract for `agents.list[].env`; after the MCP fingerprint hardening, the RED `tools.effective` regression showed an env-scoped warm catalog being hidden as stale when the read-only summary omitted `sessionKey`.

- **Target test file:** `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`, `src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`, `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.resolve-env-hook.test.ts`, `src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts`, `src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts`, `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts`, `src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts`, `src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** A selected agent defines safe env plus dangerous `NODE_OPTIONS`/`PATH`; safe env reaches real child processes and dangerous overrides stay absent. A warm MCP runtime with an env-scoped fingerprint remains visible in `tools.effective` instead of producing `mcp-stale-catalog`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The tests cover each subprocess boundary touched by the public contract and the live proof starts real spawned child processes; broader gateway startup proof would not prove this non-startup env propagation issue.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran focused Vitest shards for config/agent/CLI/exec/ACP/ACPX/MCP/`tools.effective`, ran session MCP runtime regression, ran TypeScript checks, ran whitespace diff check, and ran a local proof script that spawned direct and stdio MCP child processes.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts
Test Files: 2 passed
Tests: 42 passed

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.resolve-env-hook.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts
[test] passed 6 Vitest shards in 421.28s
Test Files: 10 passed
Tests: 267 passed

Test Files: 1 passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-post-main-sync.tsbuildinfo

Result: passed

git diff --check
Result: passed
```

- **Observed result after fix:**

```text
schema accepts agents.list[].env string maps: yes
agent config resolves env for selected agent: yes
direct spawned child OPENCLAW_AGENT_ENV=visible-to-spawned-child
direct spawned child NODE_OPTIONS=<absent>
direct spawned child PATH override=<absent>
MCP stdio spawned child OPENCLAW_AGENT_ENV=visible-to-spawned-child
MCP stdio spawned child NODE_OPTIONS=<absent>
MCP stdio spawned child PATH override=<absent>
```

- **What was not tested:** I did not use live external provider credentials or perform a production deployment. The validation is focused on the config contract, subprocess env propagation, MCP runtime/catalog behavior, TypeScript correctness, and real local child-process behavior.

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after the listed focused regressions, type checks, diff check, live child-process proof, and local ClawSweeper commit review found no actionable issues.

target.title: improve: skip Gateway migration preflight without legacy state
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`openclaw gateway run` paid the full doctor state-migration preflight on startup even when there was no legacy state to migrate. In the generic Gateway startup benchmark this kept the current-main hot path dominated by legacy state migration/import work and jiti/module-loading overhead.

## Why This Change Was Made

This change gates the Gateway foreground startup doctor migration preflight behind lightweight legacy-input detection, matching the existing read-only command behavior while preserving automatic migration for known legacy sources.

The detector covers:

- core state sidecars and pending SQLite archives
- bundled channel legacy state
- delivery queue, debug-proxy, config-health/update/current-conversation state
- cron legacy JSON/state/run-log stores, including configured `cron.store`
- shipped plugin doctor migration sources (Active Memory, Phone Control, ACPX, Device Pair, Matrix, Nostr, MSTeams, Voice Call, Memory Core, Memory Wiki, Workboard, Telegram)
- configured session stores and config-derived plugin paths
- conservative fallback when the shared-state SQLite probe cannot run

It also avoids persistent false positives from already-archived `*.migrated` plugin sources and normal current plugin-state rows.

## User Impact

Generic Gateway cold startup is substantially faster when no legacy migration input exists. Users with legacy state still get the same automatic startup migration path as before.

Benchmark artifact:

- `.artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260624T001328Z-gateway-preflight-final-candidate/before-after-summary.md`

P50 Gateway-ready deltas vs `origin/main` baseline:

| case | baseline p50 ready ms | candidate p50 ready ms | delta |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| default | 3380.7 | 1882.3 | -1498.4ms (-44.3%) |
| skipChannels | 3087.7 | 1393.6 | -1694.1ms (-54.9%) |
| oneInternalHook | 3060.8 | 1395.7 | -1665.1ms (-54.4%) |
| allInternalHooks | 3056.5 | 1391.3 | -1665.2ms (-54.5%) |
| fiftyPlugins | 3291.8 | 1587.5 | -1704.3ms (-51.8%) |
| fiftyStartupLazyPlugins | 3170.3 | 1391.3 | -1779.0ms (-56.1%) |

CPU profile evidence after the change:

- `.artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260624T001534Z-gateway-preflight-final-cpu/cpu/cpu-self-summary.md`
- skipChannels one-run: Gateway-ready 1415.8ms, CPU 2470ms
- top-profile keyword self-time: `jiti=0.0ms`, `state-migrations=0.0ms`; remaining top self-time is mostly Node package-json reads / process spawn.

## Evidence

Passed:

- `pnpm test src/cli/argv.test.ts src/cli/program/config-guard.test.ts`
  - 131 argv tests + 65 config-guard tests passed
- `pnpm build:docker`
- `pnpm check`
- `codex review --base origin/main`
  - no blocking regression identified after reviewer-driven fixes
- `pnpm test:startup:gateway --runs 5 --warmup 1 --output .artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260624T001328Z-gateway-preflight-final-candidate/gateway-startup.json`
- `pnpm test:startup:gateway --case skipChannels --runs 1 --warmup 0 --cpu-prof-dir .artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260624T001534Z-gateway-preflight-final-cpu/cpu --output .artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260624T001534Z-gateway-preflight-final-cpu/gateway-startup.json`

Validation caveat:

- Full `pnpm test` was run and failed in unrelated shards outside this patch's two changed files. Failures included provider catalog expectations, timing/process tests, backup late-SQLite tests, security scan allowlist tests, and script resolver tests. The failure log is `.artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/pnpm-test-gateway-preflight-final.log`.

target.title: fix: refresh status context window after model switch
target.body:
## Summary
- Ignore stale persisted contextTokens when /status sees a selected/default model change that is not an active fallback.
- Preserve equivalent runtime aliases while doing the stale-context check, so CLI runtime aliases keep their live context window and auth display.
- Drop cached contextTokens from the temporary live model identity used by session_status(current).
- Add regression coverage for default model switches and live current-session status.

Related to #10278, #14969, and #70692.

## Test plan
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/status.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/status/status-message.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `/status` reused a stale persisted `contextTokens` value after the selected/default model changed, so a 1m-window selected model could render as `used/200k` until the next chat turn refreshed runtime state.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on branch `codex/fix-status-stale-context-window`, using the same status renderer that backs the live `/status` output and a redacted session shape matching the reported `siderclaw-yc` stale-session state.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran `node --import tsx --eval '<buildStatusMessage with selected sider-litellm-messages/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6, stale active sider-litellm-completions/deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash, totalTokens=10000, contextTokens=200000>'` from the PR checkout.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied terminal output after the patch:

```text
🧠 Model: sider-litellm-messages/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 · 🔑 api-key
📚 Context: 10k/1.0m (1%) · 🧹 Compactions: 0
🧵 Session: agent:main:test-openclaw-sider:direct:<redacted> • updated just now
```

- Observed result after fix: the status line recomputes the selected model's 1m window and no longer renders the stale `10k/200k` value for this model-switch state; alias-equivalent runtimes still keep their active context window.
- What was not tested: no additional live provider call was made from this PR checkout; this proof covers the after-fix `/status` rendering path with redacted live-session state.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): reported live output showed `📚 Context: 57k/200k (28%)` for a selected model expected to have a 1m context window, and recovered to the expected window after a chat turn refreshed runtime state.

target.title: fix(telegram): suppress fallback reply when plugin command returns suppressReply: true
target.body:
## Summary

Plugin commands registered via `api.registerCommand` that send responses directly via Telegram Bot API (with custom retry logic, IPv4 forcing, etc.) currently trigger a spurious "No response generated. Please try again." fallback message from the Telegram channel adapter. This happens because the adapter's `hasRenderableTelegramNativeReplyPayload` check only looks for `text`/`mediaUrl` content, and when the handler returns `undefined` or `{}` after doing its own delivery, no content is found so the fallback fires.

This PR adds `suppressReply?: boolean` to `PluginCommandResult` so handlers can explicitly signal "I already took care of delivery — don't send a fallback."

## Changes

- `src/plugins/types.ts`: Add `suppressReply?: boolean` to `PluginCommandResult` with detailed JSDoc explaining when and why plugin authors should use it (for commands that deliver via channel-native APIs with custom transport guarantees)
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts`: Add `isSuppressedTelegramNativeReplyPayload` check before the fallback path; clean up progress placeholder and return early when suppressed
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts`: Regression test ensuring `{ suppressReply: true }` produces no `deliverReplies` or `editMessageTelegram` calls

Fixes #80756

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Plugin commands that own their own transport delivery via Telegram Bot API produce a spurious "No response generated. Please try again." fallback because the handler returns no content and the adapter cannot distinguish "handled externally" from "no response".

**Real environment tested:** macOS 15.4, Node v26.0.0, local OpenClaw checkout on branch fix/80756-suppress-plugin-command-reply.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. Cloned openclaw/openclaw main, created branch fix/80756-suppress-plugin-command-reply
2. Added `suppressReply?: boolean` to `PluginCommandResult` type in `src/plugins/types.ts` with JSDoc documenting the flag
3. Added `isSuppressedTelegramNativeReplyPayload` helper and early-return guard in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts`
4. Added regression test in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts`
5. Ran the full test suite for the affected file

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from running the Telegram bot-native-commands test suite locally:

```
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 /private/tmp/openclaw-main

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
```

All 19 tests passed, including the new regression test for `suppressReply: true`.

**Observed result after fix:**
- Plugin command handlers returning `{ suppressReply: true }` no longer trigger the EMPTY_RESPONSE_FALLBACK path in Telegram native commands.
- The progress placeholder is cleaned up correctly before the early return.
- Existing behavior for handlers returning `{ text: "..." }`, `{}`, or `undefined` is unchanged.
- Commit `eb3bf1a0` pushed to `fix/80756-suppress-plugin-command-reply` with the JSDoc update addressing the ClawSweeper P3 finding.

**What was not tested:** No live Telegram Bot API integration or actual message delivery was exercised; only the unit test harness behavior was validated. The fix is a pure TypeScript type + control-flow change with no runtime dependency on external APIs.

## Maintainer notes

ClawSweeper review flagged the need for live Telegram proof. This change is a pure SDK API addition + Telegram adapter control-flow fix. The regression test covers the exact code path. If a maintainer believes the test coverage is sufficient, applying `@clawsweeper re-review` or the `proof: override` label would allow this to proceed.

target.title: fix(compaction): make overflow tuning configurable
target.body:
## Summary

- add `agents.defaults.compaction.preemptiveOverflowRatio` for Pi tool-loop preemptive overflow threshold tuning
- add `agents.defaults.compaction.maxOverflowAttempts` for Pi overflow compaction retry tuning
- preserve current defaults (`0.9` ratio, `3` overflow retries) and document schema labels/help text

Fixes #80638
Fixes #80639

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Pi compaction overflow tuning is now configurable instead of hardcoded: configured values are accepted by the config schema and used by runtime resolver helpers, while unset config preserves the old defaults.
- **Real environment tested:** Harry's Mac mini, Darwin 25.4.0 arm64, Node v22.22.0, local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/80638-80639-compaction-knobs`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx - <<'EOF'
import { resolveMaxOverflowCompactionAttempts, resolvePreemptiveOverflowRatio } from './src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-config.ts';
import { AgentDefaultsSchema } from './src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts';

const config = { agents: { defaults: { compaction: { preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.72, maxOverflowAttempts: 1 } } } };
const parsed = AgentDefaultsSchema.parse(config.agents.defaults);
console.log('runtime preemptiveOverflowRatio:', resolvePreemptiveOverflowRatio(config));
console.log('runtime maxOverflowAttempts:', resolveMaxOverflowCompactionAttempts(config));
console.log('schema preserved preemptiveOverflowRatio:', parsed.compaction?.preemptiveOverflowRatio);
console.log('schema preserved maxOverflowAttempts:', parsed.compaction?.maxOverflowAttempts);
console.log('default preemptiveOverflowRatio:', resolvePreemptiveOverflowRatio({}));
console.log('default maxOverflowAttempts:', resolveMaxOverflowCompactionAttempts({}));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the real local checkout:

```text
runtime preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.72
runtime maxOverflowAttempts: 1
schema preserved preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.72
schema preserved maxOverflowAttempts: 1
default preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.9
default maxOverflowAttempts: 3
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Configured values (`0.72`, `1`) are parsed and resolved at runtime; default behavior remains `0.9` and `3` when unset.
- **What was not tested:** A live long-running overflow failure against a paid provider; validation used the real config/runtime code paths directly.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-config.test.ts src/config/config.compaction-settings.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts`
- `pnpm -s config:schema:check`
- `pnpm -s check:test-types`

target.title: fix(cli): differentiate gateway-restart hint for hot-loadable agent config sets (#80722)
target.body:
## Summary

- `config set`, `config patch`, and `config unset` now derive their post-write hint from the shared gateway reload plan instead of always printing `Restart the gateway to apply.`
- Hot-reloadable config paths, including `agents.list.*`, `agents.defaults.models.*`, and `models.*` runtime-policy edits, now print `Change will apply without restarting the gateway.`
- Restart-required paths still keep `Restart the gateway to apply.`; mixed batches keep the restart hint if any touched path requires restart.
- Legacy whole-agent runtime keys such as `agents.list[].agentRuntime` no longer force a restart hint, matching current runtime-selection docs/source where those keys are ignored by runtime selection.

Closes #80722.

## Why

The old CLI success text was stale after reload metadata landed: `agents.list` and model config are classified as hot-reloadable by the gateway reload plan, but CLI writes still printed the generic restart warning. That made active agent/model changes look restart-required even when they apply without a gateway restart.

The earlier implementation treated legacy per-agent `agentRuntime.id` as load-bearing. Current main has moved runtime selection to provider/model policy, so this refresh aligns the CLI hint with `buildGatewayReloadPlan()` instead of encoding legacy whole-agent runtime semantics in the CLI.

## Implementation

- Added `configApplyHintForPaths()` / `configApplyHintForOperations()` in `src/cli/config-cli.ts`.
- The helper calls `buildGatewayReloadPlan()` with the changed config paths.
- Result mapping:
  - `restartGateway: true` -> `Restart the gateway to apply.`
  - hot reasons present -> `Change will apply without restarting the gateway.`
  - no hot/restart reason -> `No gateway restart needed.`
- `runConfigOperations()` and direct `runConfigUnset()` use the helper for success text.
- Added regression coverage for active agent model writes, legacy whole-agent `agentRuntime`, provider runtime policy, restart-required gateway paths, and mixed hot+restart batches.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The CLI used to print `Restart the gateway to apply.` after hot-reloadable config writes such as active agent model updates. After this patch, the CLI prints the hot-reload hint for hot paths while keeping the restart hint for restart-required paths.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `0b18f14e4b7fa699f82eae9a98a3a6e81ca828a4`, using the repository's real `node scripts/run-node.mjs config` command against a temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-80722.fpLUa4/openclaw.json` fixture.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```console
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-80722.fpLUa4/openclaw.json node scripts/run-node.mjs config set 'agents.list[0].model.primary' '"openai/gpt-5.4-mini"' --strict-json
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-80722.fpLUa4/openclaw.json node scripts/run-node.mjs config set 'agents.list[1].model.primary' '"openai/gpt-5.4-mini"' --strict-json
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-80722.fpLUa4/openclaw.json node scripts/run-node.mjs config set 'models.providers.openai.agentRuntime.id' '"pi"' --strict-json
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-80722.fpLUa4/openclaw.json node scripts/run-node.mjs config set 'gateway.auth.mode' 'token'
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-80722.fpLUa4/openclaw.json node scripts/run-node.mjs config unset 'agents.list[1].agentRuntime'
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from those live CLI commands:

```console
Updated agents.list.0.model.primary. Change will apply without restarting the gateway.
Updated agents.list.1.model.primary. Change will apply without restarting the gateway.
Updated models.providers.openai.agentRuntime.id. Change will apply without restarting the gateway.
Updated gateway.auth.mode. Restart the gateway to apply.
Removed agents.list[1].agentRuntime. Change will apply without restarting the gateway.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Active-agent model writes, legacy whole-agent runtime removal, and provider runtime-policy edits now avoid the stale restart hint; the restart-required gateway auth path still prints `Restart the gateway to apply.`.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a full long-lived gateway reload loop here; this PR only changes the CLI post-write success hint and reuses the existing gateway reload planner for the classification.

### Current-head proof update (2026-06-20)

After the latest direct-unset change, I verified the broad root removals that ClawSweeper called out on head `01e96400a197`.

`config unset models` with `models.pricing.enabled` present:

```console
$ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-80823-proof-models.HdGPd1/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-80823-proof-models.HdGPd1/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_NO_COLOR=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs config unset models
Removed models. Restart the gateway to apply.
```

`config unset plugins` with `plugins.load.paths` present:

```console
$ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-80823-proof-plugins.gWf5XB/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-80823-proof-plugins.gWf5XB/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_NO_COLOR=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs config unset plugins
Removed plugins. Restart the gateway to apply.
```

The first live proof attempt exposed the root cause: whole-object removals collapsed to `models` / `plugins` before reload planning, so restart-owned descendants such as `models.pricing.*` and `plugins.load.*` could be hidden. The latest patch expands whole-object add/remove diffs to their descendant paths before calling `buildGatewayReloadPlan()`; config write behavior is unchanged.


## Verification

```console
$ pnpm exec oxfmt --write src/cli/config-cli.ts src/cli/config-cli.test.ts --threads=1
Finished in 22ms on 2 files using 1 threads.

$ OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts src/cli/config-cli.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  105 passed (105)

$ git diff --check
# exit 0
```

## Compatibility / risk

Low behavior risk: this only changes CLI success text after config writes. It does not change config parsing, validation, file writes, reload behavior, or gateway runtime selection. The hint now follows the same reload planner used by the gateway, so future reload-rule changes are not duplicated in the CLI.



## Current-head refresh, 2026-06-21

Updated this branch against current `main` to clear the red CI paths on head `da00cc2d36be5599ef9338f95123bdd6e1eb4fe1`. The PR-visible diff after the merge is still limited to:

- `src/cli/config-cli.ts`
- `src/cli/config-cli.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`

Verification on head `da00cc2d36be5599ef9338f95123bdd6e1eb4fe1`:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts src/cli/config-cli.test.ts
PASS: 1 file, 126 tests

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts
PASS: 3 files, 228 tests

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts test/scripts/openclaw-cross-os-release-checks.test.ts --reporter=verbose
PASS: 2 files, 177 tests

env NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.full-core-support-boundary.config.ts
PASS: 12 files, 97 tests

env ADDITIONAL_CHECK_GROUP=boundaries OPENCLAW_ADDITIONAL_BOUNDARY_SHARD=2/4,3/4,4/4 OPENCLAW_ADDITIONAL_BOUNDARY_CONCURRENCY=4 OPENCLAW_EXTENSION_BOUNDARY_CONCURRENCY=6 node scripts/run-additional-boundary-checks.mjs
PASS: 15/21 additional boundary checks for shard 2/4,3/4,4/4

git diff --check
```

target.title: fix(mattermost): channel prompts describe rooms as group chats
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes Mattermost channel prompt guidance so channel conversations are described consistently as channels, not partially as group chats.

- Fix classification: Root-cause prompt builder invariant fix.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High.
- Root cause: The shared group/channel prompt helpers receive trusted `ChatType: "channel"` for Mattermost channel rooms, but delivery, participant, task, tool-only, and first-turn activation guidance still had group-chat-only labels. That source prompt surface produced contradictory channel-vs-group instructions.
- Why this is root-cause fix: The fix changes the source prompt builder invariant so trusted `ChatType` selects one channel/group label set before any guidance is appended. Every trusted guidance fragment now consumes that source label set, so the prompt cannot mix `Mattermost channel` with `this group chat` for channel sessions.
- Patch quality notes: Small focused patch quality guardrail: one source file plus focused regression coverage, no catch-all fallback, no string-scrubbing downstream, and no schema/config/protocol changes.

## Linked context

Related: #95645

The issue reports that Mattermost public/private channels receive prompt intro wording that calls the conversation a group chat even though the authoritative metadata identifies the room as a channel.

Linked-open-PR scan note: external linked PRs were treated as advisory only; this change stays narrow and does not touch public API, config, schema, protocol, delivery routing, or Mattermost event ingestion.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users relying on Mattermost channel auto-reply behavior would get system prompt guidance that mixed channel wording with group-chat delivery instructions when the incoming room was a channel.

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost channel prompts now consistently describe the visible destination as a channel, including normal text delivery, message-tool delivery, participant behavior, delegated task guidance, no-visible-response guidance, and first-turn activation guidance.
- Why it matters / User impact: The model receives a single trusted description of the conversation type, avoiding contradictory prompt context that could make a channel response sound or behave like a group-chat response.

## Why This Change Was Made

Group and channel conversations share the same helper. The safest boundary is to keep that routing unchanged and make the helper derive all destination labels from trusted `ChatType` once.

- What did NOT change: This does not change message delivery routing, Mattermost event handling, session state, permissions, provider adapters, public API, config, schema, protocol, defaults, direct conversation behavior, or the wording for existing group conversations.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: `ChatType` remains the source of truth for channel-vs-group prompt wording. The PR does not add a second classifier or infer channel semantics from provider-specific strings.

## User Impact

Mattermost channel replies now get consistent channel wording throughout the system prompt, including always-on activation text. Existing group conversations keep the previous group-chat wording.

## Real behavior proof

I ran a runtime prompt capture against the patched PR head using the same Mattermost channel-type mapper, group/channel prompt builder, and first-turn activation intro that feed the auto-reply system prompt.

- Runtime input: Mattermost `channel_type=O`.
- Runtime mapping observed: `ChatType: "channel"`.
- Runtime prompt path observed: `buildGroupChatContext(...) + buildGroupIntro(...)` for a Mattermost channel first turn.
- Observed result after fix: normal delivery, message-tool-only delivery, participant guidance, delegated task guidance, no-visible-response guidance, and always-on activation all describe the room as a channel. The runtime check also rejects the former group-specific fragments `Mattermost group chat`, `this group chat`, `same group`, `group-chat task`, `group response`, and `every group message` on the channel path.
- Proof limitation: I did not attach this run to a live Mattermost server transcript in this environment; the proof below is copied runtime prompt output for the same Mattermost channel prompt path ClawSweeper requested.

Evidence after fix: Validation `mattermost-channel-combined-runtime-prompt` (pass, exit_code=0, 1127ms):

```text
Mattermost runtime input: channel_type=O
Mapped ChatType: channel
Combined normal first-turn prompt:
You are in a Mattermost channel. Your text replies are automatically sent to this channel. For ordinary text, do not use the message tool to send to this same channel; just reply normally. Use message(action=send) only when you need to send files, images, or other attachments to this same channel/topic. Be a good channel participant: mostly lurk and follow the conversation; reply only when directly addressed or you can add clear value. Emoji reactions are welcome when available. Write like a human. Avoid Markdown tables. Minimize empty lines and use normal chat conventions, not document-style spacing. Don't type literal \n sequences; use real line breaks sparingly. If addressed to someone else, stay silent unless invited or correcting key facts. When subagent or session-spawn tools are available and a directly requested channel task will require several tool calls, prefer delegating bounded side investigations early so the channel gets a responsive path forward. Keep the critical path local, avoid subagents for simple one-step work, and only surface concise channel-visible updates when they add value. If no response is needed, reply with exactly "NO_REPLY" (and nothing else) so OpenClaw stays silent. Be extremely selective: reply only when directly addressed or clearly helpful. Do not add any other words, punctuation, tags, markdown/code blocks, or explanations. If you only react or otherwise handle the message without a text reply, your final answer must still be exactly "NO_REPLY". Never say that you are staying quiet, keeping channel noise low, making a context-only note, or sending no channel reply. Any prose describing silence is wrong; the whole final answer must be only "NO_REPLY".

Activation: always-on (you receive every channel message). Address the specific sender noted in the message context.
Combined message-tool-only first-turn prompt:
You are in a Mattermost channel. Normal final replies are private and are not automatically sent to this channel. To post visible output here, use the message tool with action=send; the target defaults to this channel. Be a good channel participant: mostly lurk and follow the conversation; reply only when directly addressed or you can add clear value. Emoji reactions are welcome when available. Write like a human. Avoid Markdown tables. Minimize empty lines and use normal chat conventions, not document-style spacing. Don't type literal \n sequences; use real line breaks sparingly. If addressed to someone else, stay silent unless invited or correcting key facts. When subagent or session-spawn tools are available and a directly requested channel task will require several tool calls, prefer delegating bounded side investigations early so the channel gets a responsive path forward. Keep the critical path local, avoid subagents for simple one-step work, and only surface concise channel-visible updates when they add value. If no visible channel response is needed, do not call message(action=send). Your normal final answer stays private and will not be posted to the channel.

Activation: always-on (you receive every channel message). Address the specific sender noted in the message context.
Combined runtime prompt check: PASS
```

Additional validation on this PR head includes the new group-intro e2e regression, focused auto-reply prompt/inbound metadata tests, Mattermost monitor tests, `git diff --check`, and ClawSweeper commit review listed below.

## Evidence

The real behavior proof and validation output above are the maintainer-visible evidence for this source-repro prompt bug.

## Tests and validation

- Target test files: `src/auto-reply/reply/groups.test.ts` and `src/auto-reply/reply.triggers.group-intro-prompts.cases.ts`, with related focused coverage in `src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts` and Mattermost monitor tests.
- Scenario locked in: A Mattermost channel event with trusted `ChatType: "channel"` builds the shared group/channel context and first-turn intro, and must receive channel-specific wording throughout the prompt.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The test checks both positive channel wording and negative group-wording exclusions in normal and tool-only paths, directly guarding the source helper without adding brittle downstream prompt scrubbing.

## Risk checklist

- Risk labels considered: automation, message-delivery, security-boundary, session-state.
- Risk explanation: The changed strings describe whether replies are visible, private, or sent through the message tool, so they sit near delivery and automation guidance even though they are prompt text only.
- Why acceptable: The patch changes only prompt labels derived from the already-trusted `ChatType`; it does not alter routing, sending, session persistence, permissions, authentication, provider adapters, config, schema, protocol, or defaults.
- Compatibility/default behavior: Existing group conversations keep the previous group-chat wording, and non-channel chat types continue through the existing group path unchanged.
- Out of scope: No Mattermost event ingestion changes, no metadata shape changes, no public API/config/schema changes, no delivery semantics changes, and no prompt behavior changes for direct conversations.

## Official template answers

### What problem does this PR solve?

Mattermost channel prompts no longer mix channel wording with group-chat delivery guidance.

### Why does this matter now?

The issue is user-visible in the prompt context for Mattermost channel rooms and can give the model contradictory instructions about where replies are posted.

### What is the intended outcome?

Root-cause fix: the source prompt builder derives all group/channel wording from trusted `ChatType` before appending guidance.

### What is intentionally out of scope?

Changing Mattermost ingestion, delivery routing, session persistence, provider adapters, public API, config, schema, protocol, defaults, or direct-chat behavior is intentionally out of scope.

### Which commands did you run?

`node --import tsx --input-type=module -e '<combined Mattermost channel first-turn prompt capture>'`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply.triggers.trigger-handling.targets-active-session-native-stop.e2e.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/groups.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-gating.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts`; `git diff --check`.

### What regression coverage was added or updated?

Updated `src/auto-reply/reply/groups.test.ts` and the shared group-intro prompt cases to lock Mattermost `ChatType: "channel"` wording for normal, tool-only, and first-turn activation paths, including negative checks against the former group-specific fragments.

### What is the highest-risk area?

The highest-risk area is prompt text adjacent to automation and message-delivery guidance.

### How is that risk mitigated?

The patch changes only labels selected from trusted `ChatType`, preserves existing routing and delivery semantics, and includes focused positive/negative regression checks for both prompt helper output and first-turn activation intro plus existing Mattermost monitor tests.

### What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review.

### Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

ClawSweeper requested real behavior proof beyond mock/test-only output; the updated body now includes copied runtime prompt output for a Mattermost `channel_type=O` channel path plus the channel/group fragment checks.

## Current review state

Ready for maintainer review.

Local validation is passing, maintainer-visible proof is included above, and ClawSweeper commit review found no actionable issues.

- RF-001: Addressed by the `mattermost-channel-combined-runtime-prompt` terminal capture showing the patched combined first-turn runtime prompt for `ChatType: "channel"`, including activation intro.
- RF-002: Addressed by adding maintainer-visible runtime output beyond focused unit tests and diff checks.
- RF-003: Addressed by replacing the mock/test-only proof gap with copied prompt output from the actual Mattermost channel mapping plus prompt builder and activation intro path.
- RF-004: Disclosed as a maintainer-selection advisory; this PR remains the complete channel/group-label prompt path and does not close or mutate sibling PRs.
- RF-005: Contributor proof is now supplied and the local ClawSweeper activation-intro follow-up has been addressed; sibling PR selection remains a maintainer decision.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review finding from source: the PR runtime schema rejects invalid/reserved env keys, but the manifest JSON schema only constrains values to strings. The requested env passthrough itself is a feature request, not a broken existing behavior contract.

target.title: feat(openshell): add non-secret env config for sandbox creation
target.body: ## What Problem This Solves OpenShell sandboxes often need non-sensitive environment variables (API base URLs, model names, regions) at creation time. The OpenShell CLI supports `--env KEY=VALUE` since v0.0.59, but the OpenClaw plugin config had no way to pass these through, forcing users to manually configure env vars after each sandbox recreation. ## Why This Change Was Made Added an optional `env` map to the OpenShell plugin config. When a sandbox is created, each entry is translated to `--env KEY=VALUE` in the `openshell sandbox create` invocation. Empty by default (`{}`), so existing configs are unaffected. Env keys are validated at the schema level: - Empty keys are rejected - Keys prefixed with `OPENSHELL_` are rejected (reserved by OpenShell) - Keys must match `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*` (valid shell identifiers) - Values are arbitrary strings ## User Impact Users can configure non-secret env vars in `openclaw.json`: ```json5 { plugins: { entries: { openshell: { enabled: true, config: { env: { MODEL_BASE_URL: "https://api.example.com/v1", MODEL_NAME: "gpt-oss-20b", }, }, }, }, }, } ``` Changing env requires sandbox recreation (openclaw sandbox recreate --all). ## Evidence - Tests: 10/10 config tests pass (schema validation, env passthrough, defaults, key validation) - Lint: oxlint — no errors - Codex review findings addressed: - [P1] Env key validation added (rejects empty keys, OPENSHELL_ prefix, invalid identifiers) - [P1] Docs updated: env changes require sandbox recreation - [P2] Schema rejects invalid keys at parse time, not at sandbox create - Files changed: 5 files, +113 lines - extensions/openshell/src/config.ts — type + Zod schema with key validation + resolver - extensions/openshell/src/backend.ts — --env args in sandbox create - extensions/openshell/openclaw.plugin.json — manifest schema + uiHints - extensions/openshell/src/config.test.ts — test coverage - docs/gateway/openshell.md — config table, example, recreate guidance - Closes: #96056

target.title: feat(whatsapp): support list reply actions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: WhatsApp interactive list prompts expose stable row ids, but agents could not see or select them reliably.
- Why it matters: business and service flows often require a real WhatsApp `singleSelectReply.selectedRowId`, not a plain-text answer.
- What changed: extract inbound list rows as readable text and untrusted structured context, count list prompts as inbound content, add `list-reply`, preserve quote metadata, support native-flow/button-backed list shapes, and keep existing WhatsApp `upload-file` action routing intact.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway protocol change, no auth or credential behavior change, and no config schema change.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83217
- Related #73823
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: WhatsApp native-flow/list prompts are extracted into `Options:` rows with `rowId`, and the agent can answer them with `message(action="list-reply")`.

Real environment tested: Live linked WhatsApp session against Meta's public Jasper's Market sample and a second real WhatsApp Business list flow with identifiers redacted.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Sent `Get started` to Meta's public Jasper's Market sample number: `+15558813169`.
   Public test link: https://api.whatsapp.com/send/?phone=15558813169&text=Get+started&type=phone_number&app_absent=0
2. Ran OpenClaw with the patched WhatsApp plugin installed and the gateway linked.
3. Confirmed the inbound native-flow list rendered as `Options:` rows with `rowId`.
4. Let the agent choose a visible option using `message(action="list-reply")`.

Equivalent OpenClaw trigger command:

```bash
openclaw message send --channel whatsapp --target +15558813169 --message "Get started" --json
```

Evidence after fix: Copied redacted live output from a real linked WhatsApp session.

Video proof using Meta's public Jasper's Market sample flow only:

Before:
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/09d1206a-12ed-4c0e-a4ee-699c95fd7cdc

After:
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/993dece2-bd9b-4286-bb99-e3ee9488fc39

The before video shows the public Jasper's Market WhatsApp list prompt being treated as plain text, leading to normal `message(action="send")` replies. The after video shows OpenClaw exposing `Options:` with `rowId`, the agent calling `message(action="list-reply")`, and the gateway sending a native WhatsApp list reply. No private business flow is included.

```text
Inbound prompt:
Welcome to Jasper's Market! What can we help you with today?
Options:
1. Shop online (rowId: reply-interactive-with-media)
2. Get recipe ideas (rowId: reply-media-card-carousel)
3. Current promo (rowId: reply-offer)

Tool call:
message(action="list-reply", selectedRowId="reply-interactive-with-media", title="Shop online")

Tool result:
{ "ok": true, "selectedRowId": "reply-interactive-with-media" }
```

Additional redacted live list-flow proof:

```text
Inbound prompt:
Options:
1. [redacted visible list option] (rowId: [redacted-row-id])
2. [redacted visible list option] (rowId: [redacted-row-id])

Tool call:
message(action="list-reply", selectedRowId="[redacted-row-id]", title="[redacted visible list option]")

Tool result:
{ "ok": true, "selectedRowId": "[redacted-row-id]" }
```

Observed result after fix: the agent saw native WhatsApp list rows as structured options and sent a native list reply that the WhatsApp runtime accepted.

What was not tested: successful completion of every downstream business workflow after the selected list item; the proof covers inbound list extraction and native list-reply delivery.

Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Issue #83217 documents the missing current-main support and includes example WhatsApp list screenshots.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/message-action-spec.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-message-action.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/extract.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound.media.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/extract.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-message-action.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound.media.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-message-action.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-spec.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm build`

Latest local proof after review-blocker patch: 4 focused files passed, 75 tests passed; `git diff --check`, `pnpm check:changed`, and `pnpm build` passed.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A. This is a new WhatsApp feature request rather than a regression in existing behavior.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/extract.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-message-action.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound.media.test.ts`, `src/infra/outbound/message-action-spec.test.ts`, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: direct and wrapped list prompt extraction, native-flow list extraction, structured context propagation, content gating, canonical message action schema, row id/title validation, outbound list/button reply payload shape, quote metadata preservation, upload-file routing preservation, plugin-owned aliases, and redacted outbound list-reply logs.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior spans plugin extraction, action discovery/dispatch, and the Baileys send seam without needing a live WhatsApp account for deterministic payload proof.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A before this change.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

WhatsApp now advertises and supports a native `list-reply` message action for inbound list prompts. Agents should pass `selectedRowId` and the visible row `title`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
WhatsApp list prompt -> readable text only / no row-id action -> agent sends plain text or stalls

After:
WhatsApp list prompt -> readable options + untrusted structured context -> list-reply action -> WhatsApp singleSelectReply.selectedRowId
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This adds a WhatsApp `list-reply` message action under the existing message tool. It is gated by the existing WhatsApp `actions.sendMessage` gate plus account/allowlist authorization, requires an explicit `selectedRowId` and visible `title`, treats inbound list context as untrusted, keeps target aliases plugin-owned, and avoids logging row titles, descriptions, or selected row ids.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: local developer environment (redacted)
- Runtime/container: Node 22, pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp plugin
- Relevant config (redacted): WhatsApp channel test configs and mocked active listener/send APIs

### Steps

1. Run the focused WhatsApp/action tests listed above.
2. Run `git diff --check`.
3. Run `pnpm check:changed`.
4. Run `pnpm build`.
5. For public live reproduction, message Jasper's Market at `+15558813169` with `Get started`, then verify the agent sees `Options:` rows and uses `message(action="list-reply")`.

### Expected

- Tests pass, changed checks pass, build passes, no whitespace errors are reported, and live list prompts expose selectable `rowId` values.

### Actual

- All listed local commands exited 0. Redacted live output showed the Jasper native-flow prompt rendered with `Options:` and a `list-reply` tool result returned `ok: true`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: inbound list extraction including native-flow and button-backed prompts, list response text rendering, message action discovery/schema, action dispatch, outbound list/button reply payload shapes, quote metadata preservation, upload-file routing preservation, plugin-owned target aliases, SDK export guardrails, docs entry, public Jasper's Market video proof, and redacted live WhatsApp Business sample flow logs.
- Edge cases checked: missing selected row id, missing title, missing target, disabled `sendMessage` action, LID-backed quote metadata, unsupported camelCase alias, wrapped list messages, native-flow list prompts, button-backed prompts, synthetic button/template/native-flow response text, and list-reply log redaction.
- What you did **not** verify: a broad matrix of other WhatsApp Business providers, group chats, and every native-flow button variant beyond the covered list/button shapes.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Live WhatsApp Business list flows may require provider-specific nuance not captured by mocked Baileys tests.
  - Mitigation: The implementation uses Baileys' native `listReply`/`buttonReply` payload shapes, preserves quote metadata, keeps the action behind existing send gating, and includes focused seam tests around payload construction plus public Jasper sample proof.
